1.history: The temple of Sri Ranganathaswamy at Srirangam, the sacred island town set amid the Kaveri in the Chola country, is revered as the foremost of the 108 Divya Desams and is honoured in Srivaishnava tradition as Bhuloka Vaikuntam, the Vaikuntam upon earth. Here the Lord reposes as Sri Ranganatha, the Moolavar, in the recumbent posture upon the serpent Adisesha, facing south toward Lanka, an attitude tradition associates with his grace shown to the devotee-king of the south. According to the sthala purana, the self-manifest image was worshipped by Brahma and later by the solar dynasty, and was borne to the south by Vibhishana, brother of Ravana, who wished to install it in Lanka; the Lord chose instead to abide forever on the island of Srirangam, granting Vibhishana the boon that he would gaze ever upon him. No other Divya Desam is so abundantly sung in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham: it is glorified by nearly the whole company of the Alwars, the first three Mudhal Alwars Poigai, Bhoothath and Pey, together with Thirumazhisai Alwar, Nammalwar, Kulasekara Alwar, Periyalwar, Andal, Thondaradippodi Alwar, Thiruppaan Alwar and Thirumangai Alwar. Thiruppaan Alwar, tradition holds, sang his Amalanaadhipiraan here, beholding the Lord from foot to crown and desiring to see nothing more thereafter, while Thondaradippodi Alwar served the Lord through his flower garden and his Thirumaalai and Thiruppalliyezhuchchi. Srirangam stands as the spiritual capital of the Sri Vaishnava sampradaya, sanctified by the later acharyas, and its grandeur, vast concentric enclosures and the great festivals of the recumbent Ranganatha continue to draw pilgrims as the very heart of the Divya Desam tradition.

2.history: The temple of Azhagiya Manavalan at Uraiyur, the ancient Chola capital near Srirangam, is counted among the 108 Divya Desams of the Srivaishnava tradition. The presiding deity, the Moolavar, is worshipped as Azhagiya Manavala Perumal, the beautiful bridegroom, a name that evokes the Lord in his aspect as the divine consort. Uraiyur is cherished in tradition as the birthplace of Andal's spiritual lineage and, more particularly, as the place associated with the goddess Kamalavalli Nachiyar, whom tradition holds to have been a Chola princess who became the bride of the Lord of Srirangam; Uraiyur is thus revered as a Nachiyar sthala, intimately linked to the divine marriage celebrated at nearby Srirangam. The sacred site finds its place in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham through the hymns of Thirumangai Alwar, the warrior-saint whose verses garlanded so many of the Divya Desams of the Chola land. Through his praise, this abode of the beautiful bridegroom is enshrined among the sung temples of the Alwars, and pilgrims who tread the holy circuit of the Kaveri delta honour Uraiyur as a place of bridal devotion, where the soul, like the bride, seeks union with the Lord.

3.history: The temple of Purushothaman at Thirukkarambanur, popularly known as Uttamar Koil, in the Chola country near the confluence of the Kaveri, is reckoned among the 108 Divya Desams sung by the Alwars. The presiding deity, the Moolavar, is worshipped as Purushothama, the Supreme Person, a title of Vishnu signifying the highest among beings; from this exalted name the shrine derives its familiar appellation of Uttamar Koil, the temple of the foremost one. The site is distinguished in tradition for sheltering within its precincts the shrines of the divine consorts and the trinity, and it is cherished by devotees who hold that worship here bestows the grace of the Lord together with his Mother Lakshmi. The temple takes its place in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham through the hymns of Thirumangai Alwar, who sang the glory of so many abodes of the Lord across the Chola land and by whose verses Thirukkarambanur is sanctified among the Divya Desams. As one of the cluster of sacred temples surrounding Srirangam, Uttamar Koil forms part of the holy circuit of the Kaveri delta, and pilgrims revere it as an abode where the Supreme Person grants his presence to those who seek refuge at his feet.

4.history: Thiruvellarai, in the Chola Nadu region near the Kaveri delta, is one of the 108 Divya Desams, the sacred abodes of Sriman Narayana celebrated by the Alwars. The presiding deity (Moolavar) is Pundarikakshan, the Lord whose eyes are likened to the lotus, and tradition reveres Him here as Senthamarai Kannan, the gracious one gazing with lotus eyes upon His devotees. The temple is counted among the most ancient of the Divya Desams, set upon a small hillock approached by a winding flight of steps, and its sanctity is held to be of great antiquity. According to the sthala purana, this is a place of long-standing penance and worship where sages and devotees sought the Lord's grace, and the perumal manifests to bless those who approach Him in surrender. The glory of Thiruvellarai is sung in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham by Periyalwar and Thirumangai Alwar, whose hymns established its standing within that sacred corpus of four thousand verses. Periyalwar, foremost in singing the protective pallandu blessing upon the Lord, and Thirumangai Alwar, the prolific saint who garlanded many of the Divya Desams with his verses, together secured for this shrine an honoured place in Srivaishnava devotion. Pilgrims revere Thiruvellarai both for the beauty of the lotus-eyed Lord and for its closeness to the great temple complexes of the region, and it remains a living centre of worship where the puranic and Prabandham heritage of the Divya Desams is preserved and honoured by generations of devotees.

5.history: Thiruanbil, situated in the fertile Chola Nadu country along the Kaveri, is one of the 108 Divya Desams sung by the Alwars in praise of Sriman Narayana. The presiding deity (Moolavar) is venerated here as Vadivazhagiya Nambi, the Lord of perfect and beautiful form, also honoured as Sundararaja, the handsome king. The very name of the place, evoking anbu or loving devotion, is cherished by the tradition as expressive of the bond of love between the Lord and His devotees. According to the sthala purana, this is a hallowed spot graced by the Lord's presence to bless sages and seekers who undertook worship and penance here, and the perumal stands in His enchanting form to draw the hearts of those who behold Him. The glory of Thiruanbil is enshrined in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham through the hymns of Thirumazhisai Alwar, one of the early Alwars renowned for the depth of his philosophical devotion and his uncompromising affirmation of the supremacy of Vishnu. His verses upon this shrine secured its place among the Divya Desams and have carried its sanctity down the centuries. As one of the Divya Desams of the Kaveri region, Thiruanbil shares in the rich devotional landscape of the Chola country, where temple after temple was sanctified by the songs of the Alwars. Devotees revere Vadivazhagiya Nambi for the beauty of His form and for the grace He bestows, and the temple endures as a cherished abode within the sacred geography of Srivaishnava pilgrimage.

6.history: Koviladi, also known as Thirukkudalur, lies in the Chola Nadu region near the confluence of sacred waters in the Kaveri belt, and is counted among the 108 Divya Desams celebrated by the Alwars. The presiding deity (Moolavar) is revered here as Appakkudathaan, the Lord associated in tradition with the offering of appam, the sweet cake presented as the distinctive prasadam of this shrine, from which the perumal takes His beloved name. According to the sthala purana, the Lord graced this place to receive the loving offering of His devotees, and the giving of appam remains the cherished mark of worship here. Thirukkudalur enjoys a notable distinction among the Divya Desams in that its glory is sung by four of the Alwars in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham, an unusual abundance of praise: Thirumazhisai Alwar, the profound early saint; Nammalwar, the foremost of the Alwars whose Thiruvaymozhi is regarded as the very essence of the Vedas; Periyalwar, who sang the protective pallandu over the Lord; and Thirumangai Alwar, who garlanded so many sacred abodes with his verses. That four Alwars should have sanctified this shrine marks it as a place of singular devotional importance within the corpus. Pilgrims revere Appakkudathaan for His grace and for the sweetness of the offering that bears His name, and the temple stands as a living testament to the Prabandham heritage, where the songs of the Alwars and the puranic glory of the place continue to draw the faithful in worship and surrender.

31.history: Perarulalan, the gracious Lord of boundless mercy, presides as the Moolavar of the temple at Thirucchemponsei Koil in the Chola country, counted among the 108 Divya Desams sung in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham. The very name of the place, evoking "refined gold" (sem-pon), reflects the tradition that this sacred ground shone with a golden radiance, and the Lord here is praised as Perarulalan, He of the great and overflowing grace. As one of the divine abodes hallowed by the Alwars, the shrine is venerated as a place where the devotee may seek the Lord's compassion and find refuge at His feet. Thirumangai Alwar, the warrior-poet who sang of more Divya Desams than any other Alwar, glorified this kshetra in his verses, securing its place within the eternal canon of Tamil Vaishnava devotion. Through his pasurams the sanctity of Thirucchemponsei Koil is preserved and recited by the faithful, who hold that the Lord's mercy descended here in fullness for the upliftment of those who surrender to Him. In keeping with the character of the Chola Nadu Divya Desams, the temple stands within the cluster of sacred shrines that the Alwars traversed in their pilgrimages of song, and tradition holds that worship offered here, in the presence of the supremely merciful Perarulalan, draws the soul nearer to the Lord. The temple thus continues to be honoured as a living centre of the Sri Vaishnava tradition, its glory rooted in the hymns of Thirumangai Alwar and in the abiding presence of the all-merciful Lord.

32.history: The temple at Thirumanimaadakkoil enshrines Nara Narayanar, worshipped here as Badri Narayanar, and is counted among the 108 Divya Desams celebrated in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham. The presiding deity recalls the sacred form of Nara and Narayana, the twin sages who, according to the puranic tradition, undertook great penance for the welfare of the world; in this aspect the Lord is venerated as Badri Narayanar, an association that links this Chola-country shrine in spirit to the great northern abode of Badrinath where Vishnu is adored in His tapasvin form. To behold the Lord here as Nara Narayanar is, by tradition, to be reminded of the divine descent that took the form of austerity and compassion for the sake of all beings. Thirumangai Alwar, the foremost among the Alwars in the number of Divya Desams he sang, glorified this kshetra in his pasurams, weaving its sanctity into the eternal corpus of Tamil Vaishnava hymnody. The name Thirumanimaadakkoil, evoking a shrine of jewelled mansions or maadams, points to the architectural splendour for which such temples are remembered, and the place is honoured as one of the abodes where the Lord made Himself accessible to His devotees. Through the verses of Thirumangai Alwar, the faithful continue to recite the glory of Badri Narayanar at this Divya Desam, holding that the worship of the Lord in His sage-like, penance-bearing form bestows grace and draws the surrendered soul toward liberation. The temple endures as a cherished centre of the Sri Vaishnava tradition within the constellation of Chola Nadu Divya Desams.

33.history: Vaikuntanathan, the Lord of Sri Vaikuntam, is the presiding deity of the temple at Thiruvaikunta Vinnagaram, one of the 108 Divya Desams sung in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham. The very name of the shrine carries the word Vinnagaram, denoting a celestial temple, a "city of the heavens," and the Lord is here adored as Vaikuntanathan, the Master of the supreme abode of Vaikunta itself. By this name and form the temple is held to manifest on earth the splendour of Paramapadam, the eternal realm of the Lord, so that devotees may glimpse in their worship the bliss of the highest heaven while yet in this world. To approach Vaikuntanathan is, according to tradition, to turn the heart toward that final goal of the soul, the deathless abode from which there is no return. Thirumangai Alwar, the warrior-saint who sang the glory of more Divya Desams than any other Alwar, hallowed this kshetra in his pasurams, securing its place among the sacred shrines recited in the eternal canon of Tamil Vaishnavism. Through his hymns the faithful continue to celebrate this temple as a place where the Lord, as the very ruler of Vaikunta, makes Himself present and accessible to those who seek refuge. The shrine, bearing the title of a heavenly city, stands among the Vinnagaram temples cherished by the tradition for their evocation of the celestial, and it endures as a revered centre of Sri Vaishnava devotion where the worship of Vaikuntanathan kindles in the devotee the longing for the Supreme Abode.

34.history: The temple at Thiruvali Thirunagari, in the Chola country, enshrines the Lord in two intimately linked sacred sites that tradition regards as a single Divya Desam. At Thiruvali the presiding deity is worshipped in his fierce, beautiful lion-man aspect as Azhagiya Singar (the comely Narasimha), while at the neighbouring Thirunagari he stands in serene grace as Vayalali Manavalan, the bridegroom-Lord amid the fertile paddy fields whose name the place itself carries. This twin abode holds a special place among the 108 Divya Desams as the birthplace and spiritual home of Thirumangai Alwar, the warrior-poet and last of the twelve Alwars, whose copious outpourings make up a large portion of the Naalayira Divya Prabandham. Tradition holds that it was here that the Lord drew Thirumangai to himself and transformed the chieftain Neelan into the great devotee Parakala, and the saint sang the glory of this his native soil with deep attachment. Kulasekara Alwar, the royal saint and author of the Perumal Tirumozhi, likewise consecrated the shrine in his hymns, so that the place is doubly hallowed in the divine corpus. Pilgrims revere Thiruvali Thirunagari both for the protective strength of Narasimha and for the tender, accessible presence of the Lord as the eternal bridegroom, and the abode remains inseparably bound, in the memory of the Srivaishnava community, to the life and conversion of Thirumangai Alwar.

35.history: Thiruthevanar Thogai, in the Chola Nadu, is counted among the 108 Divya Desams, the sacred shrines of Vishnu hymned by the Alwars, and here the presiding deity is worshipped as Madhava Perumal. The very name of the place, which may be understood as the gathering or assembly of the celestials (devas), points to the traditional understanding that the gods themselves resorted to this spot to offer worship to the Lord. As Madhava, a cherished name of Vishnu signifying the Lord of fortune and consort of Lakshmi, the deity bestows his grace upon devotees who seek him in this quiet corner of the Tamil land. The shrine owes its place in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham to Thirumangai Alwar, the prolific warrior-saint whose hymns secured for many of the lesser-known shrines of the Chola country their abiding place in the sacred canon. It lies in the cluster of Divya Desams around Thiruvali Thirunagari, the Alwar's own native region, and shares in the sanctity of that hallowed neighbourhood. Through Thirumangai Alwar's verses the Lord of Thiruthevanar Thogai is remembered and revered, and the temple continues to be honoured by the Srivaishnava tradition as one of the abodes where the Lord chose to make himself present and accessible to gods and men alike.

36.history: Thiruthetriambalam, in the Chola country, is one of the 108 Divya Desams sung by the Alwars, and its presiding deity is venerated as Sengkanmaal, a name that evokes the red-eyed Lord (Sengkan Maal), Vishnu of the lotus-tinted eyes whose compassionate gaze rests upon his devotees. The epithet recalls the ancient Tamil and Sanskrit imagery of the Lord whose eyes are reddened with love and grace for those who take refuge in him. This shrine belongs to the constellation of Divya Desams gathered near Thiruvali Thirunagari, the home country of Thirumangai Alwar, and it is through the hymns of that great saint that Thiruthetriambalam finds its honoured place in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham. Thirumangai Alwar, the last of the twelve Alwars and the most copious among them, was especially devoted to the shrines of his native Chola land, and by his sacred verse he wove this temple into the eternal garland of devotional song. Pilgrims who come to Thiruthetriambalam behold the Lord as Sengkanmaal and meditate upon the boundless mercy signified by his lotus-red eyes. The temple thus stands as a witness to the Alwar's love for the many abodes of the Lord that adorn the banks and fields of the Chola country, preserving for the Srivaishnava community one more of the sanctified seats of Vishnu's grace upon the earth.

37.history: The Varadaraja Perumal Temple at Thirumanikkudam, in the Chola Nadu region, is revered as one of the 108 Divya Desams, the sacred abodes of Sri Maha Vishnu celebrated by the Alwar saints in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham. The presiding deity (Moolavar) is Varadaraja Perumal, the Lord who graciously bestows boons upon those who seek refuge at His feet, the very name Varada signifying "the giver of blessings." The place-name Thirumanikkudam, evoking a vessel or assembly of precious gems, points in tradition to the luminous sanctity attributed to this shrine. The glory of this Divya Desam was sung by Thirumangai Alwar, the great prince among the Alwars renowned for the wide compass of his pilgrimage and the depth of his devotional verse; through his hymns the temple was woven into the sacred geography of the Prabandham and assured its place among the divine abodes worshipped by the Srivaishnava tradition. As with the cluster of shrines that adorn the fertile Cauvery delta of the Chola country, this temple stands as a place where the Lord is held to remain ever-accessible to His devotees, dispensing grace to all who approach. Pilgrims who undertake the circuit of the Divya Desams come here to behold Varadaraja Perumal and to recall the verses through which Thirumangai Alwar enshrined this holy place in the memory of the faithful, honouring it as a perpetual seat of the Lord's saving compassion.

38.history: The Annan Perumal Temple at Thiruvellangkulam, popularly known as Annan Koil, in the Chola Nadu region, is counted among the 108 Divya Desams sung in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham. The presiding deity (Moolavar) here is venerated affectionately as Annan Perumal, the "elder brother," a name expressive of the intimate, familial bond that tradition draws between the Lord and His devotees at this shrine. The place-name Thiruvellangkulam, recalling a sacred white-watered tank, lends the locale its enduring identity, and the temple is widely known by the cherished name Annan Koil. This Divya Desam was hallowed by the hymns of Thirumangai Alwar, the foremost of the singing pilgrims, whose Prabandham verses secured for the shrine its place in the sacred canon of the Srivaishnava tradition. Situated amid the temple-rich plains of the Cauvery delta in the Chola country, Annan Koil belongs to a constellation of nearby Divya Desams that pilgrims customarily worship together in a single sacred circuit. According to the devotional spirit of the tradition, the Lord abides here as a near and dear elder kinsman, ever-present to receive the prayers of those who come to Him. Devotees today continue to gather at this abode to offer worship to Annan Perumal and to recite the verses of Thirumangai Alwar, through whom the glory of Thiruvellangkulam was carried into the abiding memory of the faithful and preserved as one of the Lord's sacred earthly homes.

39.history: The Thamaraiyaal Kelvan Temple at Thirupparthanpalli, in the Chola Nadu region, is honoured as one of the 108 Divya Desams celebrated by the Alwars in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham. The presiding deity (Moolavar) bears the evocative name Thamaraiyaal Kelvan, "the Consort of her who dwells upon the lotus," that is, the beloved Lord of Sri Mahalakshmi, the goddess seated upon the lotus; the appellation thus extols the deity in His eternal union with Sri, the very emblem of grace and auspiciousness in Srivaishnava theology. The place-name Thirupparthanpalli, associated in tradition with Partha — Arjuna — points to the puranic remembrance of the Lord's bond with the Pandava prince, and tradition holds this to be a place sanctified by that connection. The shrine was sung by Thirumangai Alwar, the princely saint whose far-ranging pilgrimage and copious hymns brought many of the delta shrines into the sacred corpus of the Prabandham; through his verses Thirupparthanpalli took its place among the divine abodes of the Lord. Lying within the temple-strewn Cauvery country of the Chola realm, this Divya Desam stands as a seat where the Lord, ever inseparable from Lakshmi, dispenses His grace to devotees. Pilgrims who tread the path of the 108 sacred abodes come here to worship Thamaraiyaal Kelvan and to recall the hymns of Thirumangai Alwar, by whose devotion the glory of this holy place was enshrined for generations of the faithful.

40.history: The Govindaraja Perumal Temple at Chidambaram, known in the sacred geography of the Divya Desams as Thiruchitrakoodam, enshrines the Lord in a reclining posture and is counted among the 108 sacred abodes of Vishnu celebrated by the Alwars. This shrine is remarkable for standing within the great precincts of Chidambaram, the renowned kshetra of the cosmic dance, and it represents the rare confluence of Saiva and Vaishnava sanctity in a single hallowed space. Here Govindaraja Perumal reclines majestically, the presiding Moolavar of this Divya Desam, and devotees revere the shrine as one of the holiest of the Vaishnava abodes in Chola Nadu. The name Thiruchitrakoodam evokes, in tradition, the association with Chitrakoota, linking the place to the wider devotional landscape of Vishnu's manifestations. The glory of this Divya Desam was sung in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham by Kulasekara Alwar, the saintly king whose hymns breathe intense surrender and longing for the Lord, and by Thirumangai Alwar, the great poet-saint whose verses traversed and immortalised the Divya Desams across the Tamil land. Through their sacred compositions, Thiruchitrakoodam was woven into the perpetual liturgy of Srivaishnava devotion, ensuring that the reclining Govindaraja would be remembered and worshipped through the centuries. The temple remains a place of deep pilgrimage, where the reclining form of the Lord, set amid the sanctity of Chidambaram, draws devotees seeking the grace and refuge that the Alwars themselves found and proclaimed in their hymns.

41.history: The Devanatha Perumal Temple at Thiruvahindrapuram, in the central Nadu Nadu region, is revered as one of the 108 Divya Desams, the sacred abodes of Vishnu hymned by the Alwars. The presiding deity, Devanatha Perumal, the Lord of the celestials, stands as the gracious Moolavar of this kshetra, and the place is held in great esteem within the Srivaishnava tradition. According to tradition, this hallowed site rests near the slopes associated with the serpent-realm, and the very name Thiruvahindrapuram carries the sense of a place sanctified by the Lord who is sovereign over the divine beings. The glory of this Divya Desam was sung in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham by Thirumangai Alwar, whose far-ranging hymns gathered the sacred abodes of Vishnu into the enduring corpus of Tamil devotion, and through his verses Thiruvahindrapuram took its honoured place among the celebrated Divya Desams. In later Srivaishnava history this kshetra became especially dear as a place intimately connected with the life and devotion of the acharya tradition, deepening its standing as a centre of learning and surrender. Pilgrims who come to Devanatha Perumal seek the protection of the Lord of the gods, drawn by the same devotion that moved the Alwar to immortalise this place in song, and the temple continues to stand as a living testimony to the unbroken line of worship that the hymns of the Prabandham set in motion.

42.history: The temple at Thirukkovalur, honoured as Adhi Thiruvarangam, enshrines the Lord as Trivikraman, with the sanctum also revered under the name Aranganathar, and it is counted among the 108 Divya Desams celebrated by the Alwars in the central Nadu Nadu region. The presiding Moolavar stands in the majestic form of Trivikrama, recalling the cosmic stride of the Lord who, as Vamana, measured the worlds with his feet, and the kshetra is held to be one of supreme significance in the Srivaishnava tradition. Thirukkovalur is famed above all as the sacred place where, according to tradition, the first three Alwars—Poigai Alwar, Bhoothath Alwar, and Peyalwar—came together in a narrow passage during a stormy night and, drawn close in the press of darkness, perceived the presence of the Lord himself among them; from this meeting flowed the very first hymns of divine longing that opened the great outpouring of the Naalayira Divya Prabandham. Poigai Alwar and Bhoothath Alwar sang the glory of this Divya Desam, and Thirumangai Alwar too added his verses to its praise, so that the place is woven inseparably into the origins of the Prabandham corpus. As the abode bearing the title Adhi Thiruvarangam, the original or foremost Srirangam, Thirukkovalur holds a cherished place in devotional memory. Pilgrims revere it as the cradle of the Alwar tradition, where the Lord Trivikraman, who once spanned the universe, remains present to grant his grace to those who seek refuge in him.

43.history: The Varadaraja Perumal Temple at Kanchipuram, set upon the small hill known as Atthigiri (Hastagiri), enshrines the Lord as Varadaraja, the "King of Boons," and stands among the foremost of the 108 Divya Desams in the Thondai Nadu region. The presiding Moolavar, Devaraja or Varadaraja Perumal, is revered as the very embodiment of grace, the granter of every wish to those who seek his refuge. According to the sthala purana, this is the place hallowed by the great yajna of Brahma the creator, who is said to have performed a sacrifice here from which the Lord arose to bless the world, and the deity is traditionally associated with the sacred fig (atthi) wood form. Kanchipuram is celebrated in the Srivaishnava tradition as one of the seven sacred cities (mokshapuris) granting liberation, and Atthigiri is its crowning Vishnu shrine. The glory of this Divya Desam was sung by the earliest of the Alwars, Bhoothath Alwar and Pey Alwar, two of the three Mudhal Alwars whose pasurams opened the devotional outpouring of the Naalayira Divya Prabandham, and later by Thirumangai Alwar, the prince among poets. Their hymns enshrine the Lord of Atthigiri as the supreme refuge. The temple is renowned for its grand festivals and its sanctity within the Srivaishnava world, and it holds a place of singular eminence as one of the great triad of divine sites at Kanchipuram, where the devout gather to behold the Lord who showers boons upon all who come before him.

44.history: The Ashtabhuja Perumal Temple at Thiruattabuyakaram in Kanchipuram enshrines the Lord in his eight-armed (ashtabhuja) form and is counted among the 108 Divya Desams of the Thondai Nadu region. The presiding Moolavar, Ashtabhujakaram Perumal, stands bearing weapons and emblems in his eight hands, a majestic manifestation of the Lord as protector who removes the fears of his devotees and shields them from every affliction. According to the sthala purana, the Lord appeared here in this eight-armed form to grant grace and to affirm his supremacy and his readiness to defend those who take refuge in him; tradition holds that the place is associated with the redemption of a devotee through the Lord's gracious intervention. Kanchipuram, one of the seven sacred mokshapuri cities, is dense with Vishnu shrines, and Ashtabhujakaram is among its revered abodes. The glory of this Divya Desam was sung in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham by Pey Alwar, one of the three Mudhal Alwars whose pasurams stand at the very fountainhead of the Prabandham, and by Thirumangai Alwar, whose verses celebrate the Lord of the eight arms. Through their hymns the shrine is fixed in the sacred geography of the Divya Desams. The temple remains a cherished site of pilgrimage, where the worshipper beholds the Lord in his rare and powerful eight-armed posture, an enduring assurance of the Lord's protective grace toward all who seek him.

45.history: The Deepaprakasar Temple at Thiruthanka (Thooppul) in Kanchipuram enshrines the Lord as Deepaprakasar, the "Radiance of the Lamp," also known in Tamil as Vilakkoli Perumal, and is counted among the 108 Divya Desams of the Thondai Nadu region. The presiding Moolavar shines as the very light that dispels the darkness of ignorance, and his name itself proclaims the Lord as the self-luminous effulgence that illumines the path of his devotees. According to the sthala purana, the Lord manifested here as a radiant lamp to assist Brahma's great sacrifice and to bless the place with his luminous presence, and tradition holds that the deity appeared as a steadying light when darkness threatened the rites. The glory of this Divya Desam was sung in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham by Thirumangai Alwar, whose pasurams enshrine the Lord of Thiruthanka among the sacred abodes of Vishnu. Thiruthanka is further hallowed in Srivaishnava memory as a place intimately connected with the tradition of the acharyas of Kanchipuram, and it stands close to the cluster of holy Vishnu shrines that make the sacred city one of the seven mokshapuris granting liberation. The temple endures as a beloved site of pilgrimage, where the faithful come to behold the Lord whose form is itself the lamp of grace, the radiance that scatters every shadow from the hearts of those who worship him.

46.history: Thiruvelukkai, set within the sacred precincts of Kanchipuram in the ancient Thondai Nadu country, is revered as one of the 108 Divya Desams, the holy abodes of Lord Vishnu hymned by the Alwars. Here the Lord is enshrined as Azhagiya Singar, the beautiful lion-form of Narasimha, the man-lion avatara who tore apart the demon Hiranyakashipu to protect his devotee Prahlada. According to the sthala purana, the place is associated with the Lord's fierce yet gracious Narasimha manifestation, and the very name Velukkai is traditionally explained as deriving from the Lord's resolve to remain seated here, fulfilling the prayers of the celestials and sages who sought his presence. The presiding Moolavar of Narasimha here is celebrated for combining the awe of the man-lion with surpassing beauty and compassion, so that the terror of the avatara is softened into grace for those who take refuge in him. The temple's glory was sung in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham by the early saint Pey Alwar, one of the three foremost Mudhal Alwars, and by Thirumangai Alwar, the warrior-poet whose pasurams adorn so many of the shrines of this region. Their inclusion of Thiruvelukkai among the sacred hundred and eight confirms its standing in the devotional landscape of Srivaishnavism. Pilgrims who worship at this shrine, counted among the many Divya Desams clustered in and around Kanchipuram, come seeking the protection of the Lord who appears as a refuge for the helpless, the fierce destroyer of evil who yet remains, in this place, the lovely and approachable Azhagiya Singar.

47.history: Thiruneeragam, located within the sacred temple-city of Kanchipuram in Thondai Nadu, is honoured as one of the 108 Divya Desams sung by the Alwars in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham. The presiding deity, the Moolavar, is worshipped here as Neeragathan, the Lord whose very name evokes his association with sacred waters, neer meaning water in Tamil. Tradition counts this among the cluster of Divya Desams gathered in and around Kanchipuram, several of which are revered as shrines whose Lord is present in subtle and inward forms rather than always in grand independent temples. The glory of Thiruneeragam was sung by Thirumangai Alwar, the great warrior-saint whose extensive pilgrimages and copious pasurams secured for so many shrines of the Thondai country their place in the canonical corpus. By his hymning, this abode was raised into the sacred hundred and eight, and his verses preserve its memory as a place where the Lord graciously chose to dwell and bless his devotees. Faithful to the reticence of the tradition where elaborate legend is not firmly attested, the abiding significance of Thiruneeragam rests in its inclusion in the Divine Prabandham itself, for the Alwars sang only of those abodes where they beheld the living presence of the Lord. Pilgrims journeying through the Divya Desams of Kanchipuram worship Neeragathan as part of this constellation of holy shrines, receiving the grace of the Lord celebrated in the Tamil Veda of the Alwars and cherished in the devotional life of Srivaishnavas.

48.history: Thiruppadagam, within the great temple-city of Kanchipuram in Thondai Nadu, is venerated as one of the 108 Divya Desams of Lord Vishnu hymned in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham. The presiding deity here is the celebrated Pandava Doothar, the Lord enshrined in his role as the messenger and envoy of the Pandavas. According to tradition, this form commemorates Sri Krishna's mission to the court of the Kauravas on behalf of the Pandavas, when he went as a peace ambassador seeking to avert the great war, and the Lord is remembered here in that gracious office of advocate for his devotees. The Moolavar is famed as one of the largest seated images among the Divya Desams, a vast and majestic form of the Lord seated in his royal embassy, which draws the wonder and devotion of pilgrims. The exceptional sanctity of Thiruppadagam is marked by the number of Alwars who sang its praise: among the three foremost Mudhal Alwars, both Bhoothath Alwar and Pey Alwar hymned this shrine, as did the philosopher-saint Thirumazhisai Alwar and the prolific Thirumangai Alwar. This rich confluence of Alwar pasurams underscores its eminence within the corpus and among the Divya Desams of Kanchipuram. To meditate upon Pandava Doothar is to recall the Lord who lowers himself to serve and intercede for those who love him, and Thiruppadagam stands as the abode where this tender truth of his condescension is enshrined for the worship of the faithful.

49.history: The Nilathingal Thundam Perumal Temple, situated within the great Ekambareswarar Siva temple complex at Kanchipuram in the Thondai Nadu region, enshrines the Lord as Nilathingal Thunda Perumal, here revered as a Divya Desam abode of Vishnu, with the consort honoured as Nilamangai Thaayar. The name Thirunilathingal Thundam evokes a sliver of the cool moon, and the sthala is celebrated as a place where the harmony of Vishnu and Siva is made visible, the Perumal shrine standing as a Vaishnava holy seat within a renowned Saiva kshetra. As one of the 108 Divya Desams sung in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham, its glory was hymned by Thirumangai Alwar, whose pasurams set this abode among the sacred places of Kanchi. Tradition holds Kanchipuram to be a city dense with the presence of the Lord, where many Divya Desams cluster together, and Thirunilathingal Thundam takes its place among them as a shrine where devotees who come to the ancient temple may also receive the grace of Vishnu. Standing thus, the temple embodies the Srivaishnava understanding that the Lord pervades all sacred ground, and it remains an object of pilgrimage for those who walk the circuit of Kanchi's divine abodes, drawn by the praise of Thirumangai Alwar and the antiquity of the place.

50.history: The Ulagalantha Perumal Temple at Thiruooragam in Kanchipuram, Thondai Nadu, enshrines the Lord in His majestic Trivikrama form, here called Ulagalantha Perumal, the One who measured the worlds, with the consort revered as Amudhavalli Thaayar. According to the sthala purana, the temple commemorates the celebrated Vamana avatara, in which the Lord, having come as a dwarf brahmachari to the sacrifice of the asura king Mahabali and received the gift of three paces of land, grew into the cosmic Trivikrama and strode across earth and heaven to reclaim the worlds, setting His foot upon Mahabali's head and granting him grace. The towering moolavar, depicted with one leg raised aloft in the act of measuring the universe, makes this one of the great Trivikrama shrines among the Divya Desams. As one of the 108 sacred abodes sung in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham, Thiruooragam was hymned by Thirumazhisai Alwar and Thirumangai Alwar, whose pasurams extol the Lord who spanned all creation in two strides. Counted among the cluster of Divya Desams within Kanchi, the temple stands as a vivid icon of divine sovereignty and grace, and the form of Ulagalantha Perumal remains a powerful reminder of the Lord's boundless reach and His tender accessibility to the surrendered, drawing pilgrims to behold the very gesture by which the worlds were won back.

51.history: The Yathothkari Perumal Temple at Thiruvekka in Kanchipuram, Thondai Nadu, enshrines the Lord reclining as Sonna Vannam Seitha Perumal, the One who did exactly as was told, also known as Yathothkari, with the consort revered as Komalavalli Thaayar. According to the sthala purana, this is intimately bound to the life of Thirumazhisai Alwar, whose name the place carries in tradition: it is told that when the saint, slighted, prepared to leave the town, he bade the reclining Lord to roll up His serpent-bed and accompany him, and the Lord obediently did as the devotee said, until entreated to remain, the very deed from which the name Sonna Vannam Seitha Perumal arises. Tradition also links the kshetra to the great river Saraswati, restrained by Brahma's sacrifice. Among the foremost Divya Desams, Thiruvekka holds a singular distinction, for it is sung by five Alwars in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham. The three earliest mudhal Alwars, Poigai Alwar, Bhoothath Alwar and Pey Alwar, are associated with Kanchi, and here their successor Thirumazhisai Alwar, together with Nammalwar and Thirumangai Alwar, hymned the Lord's glory, in this listing represented by Poigai Alwar, Pey Alwar, Thirumazhisai Alwar, Nammalwar and Thirumangai Alwar. Thus richly praised, Thiruvekka stands as one of the most beloved abodes of Kanchi, where the Lord's loving obedience to His devotee is forever enshrined.

52.history: The Karunakara Perumal Temple at Thirukkaragam enshrines the Lord whose name proclaims him as the very ocean of grace, Karunakara, the boundless bestower of compassion (karuna). Counted among the 108 Divya Desams, the sacred abodes of Sriman Narayana celebrated by the Alwars, this temple lies in the region of Thondai Nadu, the northern Tamil country rich in ancient Vaishnava shrines. The Moolavar here is worshipped as Karunakara Perumal, the Lord who graciously turns toward his devotees and removes their sorrows. Tradition holds that the very place-name Karagam recalls the presence of the Lord in this hallowed spot, where divine mercy is said to flow freely to all who seek refuge. Among the Alwar saints, it is Thirumangai Alwar, the prince-poet of the Naalayira Divya Prabandham, who sang the glory of this temple and thereby secured its place in the sacred canon. His verses, born of a heart wholly surrendered to the Lord, garland this shrine and invite generations of devotees to behold the Karunakara, the merciful one, here. As one of the comparatively lesser-known but no less sacred Divya Desams of the Kanchipuram cluster in Thondai Nadu, Thirukkaragam holds a cherished place in the pilgrimage of the faithful, who circumambulate these abodes recalling that each was hallowed by the song of an Alwar. To meditate upon the Lord here as Karunakara is, in the tradition, to place oneself within the reach of his unfailing grace.

53.history: The Karvanar Perumal Temple at Thirukkar Vanam stands among the 108 Divya Desams, the supreme sanctuaries of Lord Vishnu glorified in the song of the Alwars. Situated in Thondai Nadu, the venerable northern Tamil region that cradles many of the most ancient Vaishnava shrines around Kanchipuram, this temple enshrines the Moolavar as Karvanar Perumal. The name evokes the dark rain-laden cloud, kar, a beloved image of the Lord in Tamil devotional poetry, for Narayana is ever likened to the cool, dark monsoon cloud that pours its bounty selflessly upon the earth; the place-name Kar Vanam itself carries this fragrance of the dark-hued divine. The Lord here is thus contemplated as the rain-cloud-coloured one, whose very form bespeaks his readiness to shower grace. This Divya Desam was hallowed by the verses of Thirumangai Alwar, that ardent saint among the twelve Alwars whose copious hymns in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham celebrate temple after temple across the sacred geography of the Tamil land. Through his song, Thirukkar Vanam was lifted into the eternal corpus of Sri Vaishnava devotion and joined the company of the 108 abodes venerated by pilgrims. As one of the closely clustered Divya Desams of the Kanchipuram region, the temple shares in the deep antiquity and sanctity of that storied land. Devotees who worship here recall that to behold the Lord as the dark, beneficent cloud is to trust in the ceaseless rain of his mercy.

54.history: The Kalvar Perumal Temple at Thirukkalvanur enshrines the Lord in his form as Adi Varaha, the primordial Boar incarnation of Sriman Narayana, and is revered as one of the 108 Divya Desams sung by the Alwars. The Moolavar here is fondly addressed as Kalvar Perumal, literally the Lord who is a 'thief,' a tender name well-loved in the Sri Vaishnava tradition, for the Lord is said to steal away the hearts of his devotees and to win them stealthily by his grace. According to the tradition associated with this shrine, the Lord here is worshipped in the aspect of Adi Varaha, recalling the great cosmic deed in which Vishnu, as the divine Boar, lifted the earth-goddess Bhudevi from the encircling waters and restored the world; thus the place is hallowed by the memory of the Lord's saving descent. Situated in Thondai Nadu within the sacred ambit of Kanchipuram, Thirukkalvanur belongs to the rich constellation of Divya Desams that adorn that ancient city. It was Thirumangai Alwar, the warrior-saint and prolific singer of the Naalayira Divya Prabandham, who sang the praise of this temple and enshrined it forever in the divine corpus. To approach the Lord here as Kalvar, the gracious 'thief' who is also Adi Varaha the rescuer of the earth, is, in the devotion of the tradition, to surrender willingly to one who lovingly captures the soul and bears it to safety.

55.history: The Pavala Vannan Temple at Thiruppavala Vannam, within the sacred precincts of Kanchipuram in Thondai Nadu, enshrines the Lord in His coral-hued form as Pavala Vannar (Pravala Varnan), the presiding Moolavar from whom the kshetra takes its very name. Counted among the 108 Divya Desams, the abodes of Vishnu hallowed by the song of the Alwars, this shrine is celebrated for the Lord's resplendent appearance, His complexion likened to gleaming coral or red coral, a vision of beauty that the devotee is invited to behold and adore. The temple stands as one of the cluster of divine sanctuaries that make Kanchipuram one of the foremost concentrations of Divya Desams, the city sometimes revered as a seat of liberation. According to tradition, the place is honoured as one where the Lord graciously manifests His auspicious form for the contemplation of His devotees. The sacred shrine received the consecration of hymn through Thirumangai Alwar, the last and most prolific of the twelve Alwars, whose pasurams in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham sing the glory of this place and secure its standing within the canonical roll of the Divya Desams. By the grace of the Alwar's verses, Pavala Vannan is remembered and worshipped in the unbroken Srivaishnava tradition, and pilgrims journeying through the Divya Desams of Thondai Nadu pause here to take darshan of the coral-coloured Lord and to seek His benevolence.

56.history: The Vaikunta Perumal Temple at Thiruparameswara Vinnagaram, in Kanchipuram of Thondai Nadu, is revered as one of the 108 Divya Desams sung by the Alwars. The very name Vinnagaram signifies a celestial city or a temple of the heavens, and the Lord here is worshipped as Vaikunta Perumal, the master of Sri Vaikuntam, evoking for the devotee a foretaste of the Supreme Abode upon earth. The presiding Moolavar grants darshan in this sanctuary as a vision of the Lord who reigns in glory, and the shrine is held among the cluster of sacred abodes that make Kanchipuram a city dense with Divya Desams. Tradition holds this temple to be an ancient and storied foundation, and it is well known for the sculptured panels of its enclosure that narrate sacred lore for the contemplation of the faithful, though pilgrims come above all to adore the Lord in His several aspects within the shrine. The kshetra was hallowed by hymn through Thirumangai Alwar, the most prolific of the twelve Alwars, whose pasurams in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham extol this Vinnagaram and confirm its place within the canonical company of the Divya Desams. By the grace of the Alwar's song, Thiruparameswara Vinnagaram is cherished in the unbroken Srivaishnava tradition, and devotees making the round of the Divya Desams of Thondai Nadu come here to behold Vaikunta Perumal and to meditate upon the bliss of the heavenly city the Lord embodies.

57.history: The Vijayaraghava Perumal Temple at Thiruputkuzhi, in Thondai Nadu near Kanchipuram, enshrines the Lord as Vijayaraghava Perumal, the victorious scion of the Raghu line, that is, Sri Rama, and is counted among the 108 Divya Desams sung by the Alwars. The place is intimately associated with the sacred legend of Jatayu, the noble vulture-king of the Ramayana who gave his life striving to rescue Sita from the demon Ravana. According to the sthala purana, it was here that Rama, journeying in search of His consort, came upon the mortally wounded Jatayu and, honouring the bird's supreme sacrifice, performed his final rites with His own hands. The very name Thiruputkuzhi is understood in tradition to recall this sacred episode of the bird, and the Lord is worshipped here in a posture associated with the grace He bestowed upon Jatayu, granting him liberation. The shrine thus stands as a place where the Lord's compassion toward even a bird is forever commemorated. Thiruputkuzhi was sanctified by hymn through Thirumangai Alwar, the last of the twelve Alwars, whose pasurams in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham celebrate this kshetra and secure its place among the canonical Divya Desams. By the grace of the Alwar's verses and the abiding memory of Jatayu's devotion, pilgrims of the Srivaishnava tradition come to Thiruputkuzhi to take darshan of Vijayaraghava Perumal and to contemplate the Lord's boundless mercy toward His devotees.

58.history: At Thiruninravur in the ancient Thondai Nadu region stands the Bhaktavatsala Perumal Temple, counted among the 108 Divya Desams sung in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham of the Alwars. The presiding deity, the Moolavar, is revered here as Bhaktavatsala Perumal, the Lord whose very name proclaims His tender affection for His devotees, drawn as a calf is drawn to its mother. The Lord is enshrined in a standing posture, and the place-name Thiruninravur itself, meaning 'the sacred town where the Lord stands,' echoes this form of grace. Tradition holds that the Lord lingers here in compassion, ever near to those who seek refuge, and the Mother is venerated alongside Him as the embodiment of intercessory grace. The glory of this kshetra was sung by Thirumangai Alwar, the warrior-poet among the twelve Alwars, whose hymns secured its place in the sacred canon of the Divya Prabandham and brought its sanctity to the wider community of Srivaishnavas. Pilgrims journeying among the Divya Desams of the Tamil land have long included Thiruninravur in their circuit, drawing near to a Lord who, according to the sthala purana, abides here chiefly to demonstrate His boundless love for His bhaktas. The temple thus stands as a living testament to the central Srivaishnava conviction that the Lord delights in His devotees and remains forever accessible to them, a truth that the name Bhaktavatsala enshrines in stone and song alike.

59.history: The Veera Raghava Swamy Temple at Thiruvallur in Thondai Nadu is one of the 108 Divya Desams glorified in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham. Here the Lord is worshipped as Veera Raghava, a form associated with Rama of the Raghu line, and He is famed especially as the divine physician (Vaithya Veeraraghava), to whom devotees turn for healing of bodily and spiritual ills. Tradition holds that the Lord here removes the afflictions of those who seek Him, and the temple is renowned across the region as a place of cure and consolation; offerings made in supplication for health are a long-standing devotional practice at this shrine. The Lord is enshrined in a reclining posture, and the Mother is venerated beside Him as the bestower of grace. The sanctity of Thiruvallur was sung by two of the Alwars, Thirumazhisai Alwar and Thirumangai Alwar, whose hymns wove this kshetra into the sacred tapestry of the Divya Prabandham. Thirumazhisai Alwar, himself closely associated with the surrounding Thondai country, gives the place a special intimacy, while Thirumangai Alwar's verses added to its renown among pilgrims. According to the sthala purana, the Lord abides here as the great healer who, like a skilled physician, diagnoses and dispels the deepest maladies of samsara. Thus Thiruvallur is cherished not only as a station on the Divya Desam pilgrimage but as a refuge of mercy, where the reclining Veera Raghava grants both worldly relief and the ultimate cure of liberation to those who take shelter at His feet.

60.history: The Parthasarathy Temple at Triplicane, the sacred Thiruvallikeni in Thondai Nadu, is among the most celebrated of the 108 Divya Desams and stands at the heart of the great city of Chennai. The presiding deity is Parthasarathy, 'the charioteer of Partha,' the form in which Lord Krishna served as Arjuna's charioteer upon the field of Kurukshetra. In keeping with this legend, the Lord here is traditionally depicted bearing marks upon His countenance, understood as the scars of the arrows received in battle while He shielded His devotee, a vivid emblem of the saulabhya, the gracious accessibility, with which He stands ever ready to protect those who seek refuge. The place-name Thiruvallikeni, the 'sacred lily pond,' lends the kshetra its gentle beauty. Three of the Alwars sang the glory of this shrine: Pey Alwar, one of the three foremost Mudhal Alwars, together with Thirumazhisai Alwar and Thirumangai Alwar, and their hymns enshrine Thiruvallikeni firmly within the Naalayira Divya Prabandham. The presence of Pey Alwar's verse links the temple to the very dawn of the Alwar tradition. According to the sthala purana, the Lord chose to manifest here in His form as the divine charioteer, and the temple has long been a flourishing centre of Srivaishnava worship and festival. Its grand celebrations and the daily devotion of countless pilgrims make Thiruvallikeni a living jewel among the Divya Desams, where the Lord who guided Arjuna continues to guide and guard all who come before Him.

7.history: The temple at Thirukkandiyur in the Chola Nadu enshrines the Lord as Harasapa Vimochana Perumal, counted among the 108 Divya Desams sung in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham. The very name of the presiding deity proclaims the legend most closely associated with this sacred place. According to the sthala purana, it was here that the Lord delivered Hara (Siva) from a grave curse and the burden of sin that clung to him, the divine name Harasapa Vimochana meaning the one who freed Hara from his curse. Tradition holds that Siva, afflicted after an act of transgression involving Brahma, found release and purification only through the grace of Vishnu at this kshetra, and so the place stands as an enduring testimony to the supreme cleansing power of the Lord's compassion. For this reason Thirukkandiyur is remembered as a place where even the gods sought refuge and were redeemed. The glory of this Divya Desam was sung by Thirumangai Alwar, whose verses in the Prabandham secured its place among the celebrated abodes of Vishnu and carried the renown of the Lord here to devotees across the generations. As one of the Divya Desams of the fertile Chola country, the temple draws pilgrims who come to behold the Moolavar and to seek the same liberation from sin that tradition says the Lord granted to Hara himself, making it a shrine especially venerated for the forgiveness and grace bestowed upon those who worship there.

8.history: Situated at Thirukkoodalur, also known as Aaduthurai, in the Chola Nadu, this temple enshrines the Lord as Vaiyam Kaatha Perumal and is honoured among the 108 Divya Desams celebrated in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham. The name of the presiding deity, Vaiyam Kaatha Perumal, means the Lord who protected the world, and it expresses the central significance of this sacred abode as a place where the Lord stands forth as the saviour and guardian of all creation. Tradition reveres the Moolavar here as the protector who shelters the earth and its beings, and the kshetra is approached by devotees seeking that same all-encompassing divine refuge. Lying in the rich Kaveri delta of the Chola country, Thirukkoodalur belongs to the cluster of Divya Desams that adorn this region sanctified by the great river, and its very location among these holy waters has long marked it as an auspicious place of worship. The glory of this Divya Desam was sung by Thirumangai Alwar, and his hymns within the Prabandham established the temple firmly among the divine abodes extolled by the Alwars, ensuring that the praise of Vaiyam Kaatha Perumal would be remembered and recited through the centuries. Pilgrims who come to this shrine venerate the Lord as the one whose protecting grace upholds the world, finding in his presence the assurance of shelter and the abiding mercy that the Alwar celebrated in his sacred verses.

9.history: The temple at Thirukkavithalam, also called Kabisthalam, in the Chola Nadu enshrines the Lord as Gajendra Varadan and stands among the 108 Divya Desams sung in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham. The name of the presiding deity directly recalls the celebrated episode of Gajendra Moksha, and according to the sthala purana this kshetra is intimately associated with that great act of divine deliverance. Tradition holds that when Gajendra, the lord of elephants, was seized by a crocodile and cried out in helpless devotion, the Lord rushed to his aid and granted him liberation, and it is in remembrance of this grace that he is worshipped here as Gajendra Varadan, the bestower of boons upon Gajendra. The temple thus embodies the timeless promise that the Lord answers the surrendered cry of his devotees, hastening to rescue those who turn to him in distress. Set within the sacred Kaveri delta of the Chola country, Thirukkavithalam takes its place among the Divya Desams that grace this hallowed region. The glory of this abode was sung by Thirumazhisai Alwar, whose verses in the Prabandham enshrined the temple among the divine abodes praised by the Alwars and carried its renown to generations of devotees. Worshippers who come before the Moolavar here meditate upon the Lord's swift and boundless compassion as revealed in the deliverance of Gajendra, finding in this shrine an abiding emblem of refuge for all who seek his protection.

61.history: Neervanna Perumal at Thiruneermalai, in the Thondai Nadu region near Chennai, is venerated as one of the 108 Divya Desams, the sacred abodes of Vishnu hymned by the Alwars. The presiding deity (Moolavar), Neervanna Perumal, takes his name from his complexion likened to water (neer), and the temple is distinctive for being spread across the foot of a small hill and its summit, so that the Lord is worshipped here in the standing, sitting, and reclining postures within the same kshetra. According to the sthala purana, the place was once surrounded by water, from which it draws its name Thiruneermalai, the sacred hill of waters. Tradition associates the kshetra with Sri Rama, who is said to have graced this hill, and the Lord here is also revered in the form of Ranganatha in repose. The Divya Desam was sung by two of the early Mudhal Alwars and Mangai saint of the Naalayira Divya Prabandham: Bhoothath Alwar, one of the three foremost Mudhal Alwars, and Thirumangai Alwar, the prolific warrior-poet whose verses enrich the glory of many Thondai Nadu shrines. Their pasurams secure Thiruneermalai's place within the four-thousand-verse corpus and mark it as a hallowed site of pilgrimage. Devotees ascend the hill to behold the Lord in his several forms, regarding the climb itself as an act of devotion and the darshan at the summit as the crown of the visit.

62.history: Nitya Kalyana Perumal at Thiruvidaventhai, on the coast of Thondai Nadu south of Chennai, is honoured among the 108 Divya Desams sung in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham. The presiding deity (Moolavar) bears the name Nitya Kalyana Perumal, the Lord of perpetual auspiciousness, and is also revered as Varaha Perumal, associated with the boar incarnation of Vishnu who lifted the earth from the cosmic waters. According to the sthala purana, the sage Kalava worshipped here and prayed that his many daughters might be wedded; the Lord is said to have married them, and from this episode arises both the name Nitya Kalyana, signifying ever-present marriage and prosperity, and the temple's enduring fame as a place where devotees seek the blessing of timely and happy marriage. Tradition holds that the Lord grants ceaseless auspicious tidings to those who pray with sincerity, and the shrine is widely sought by families desiring matrimonial good fortune. This Divya Desam was sung by Thirumangai Alwar, the warrior-saint among the twelve Alwars, whose pasurams celebrate the Lord of Thiruvidaventhai and confirm its standing within the sacred corpus. Set near the sea, the kshetra preserves the memory of the Varaha avatara and the grace bestowed upon the sage, and remains a living centre of worship where the prayer for nitya kalyanam, unending well-being, is offered before the Perumal.

63.history: Sthalasayana Perumal at Thirukkadalmallai, the celebrated coastal town of Mahabalipuram in Thondai Nadu, is reckoned among the 108 Divya Desams extolled in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham. The presiding deity (Moolavar), Sthalasayana Perumal, is distinguished by his reclining posture directly upon the ground (sthala), without the customary serpent couch of Adisesha, a rare and revered form of the Lord. According to the sthala purana, the sage Pundarika undertook intense devotion at this shore, seeking to offer lotuses to the Lord and labouring to empty the sea so as to reach him; moved by this devotion, Vishnu is said to have appeared and reclined upon the bare earth before his devotee, whence the name Sthalasayana, the one who reposes on the ground. The kshetra is twice blessed in the Prabandham, sung both by Bhoothath Alwar, one of the three Mudhal Alwars who inaugurated the Alwar tradition, and by Thirumangai Alwar, whose abundant pasurams glorify this seaside abode. Mahabalipuram itself is famed as an ancient port and a seat of sculptural splendour, and the Divya Desam stands amid this heritage as a place of singular sanctity. Pilgrims come to behold the Lord in his ground-reclining form and to recall the devotion of the sage who, by his unwavering faith, drew the Lord to rest upon the very land of Thirukkadalmallai.

64.history: Perched atop the larger of the two hills at Sholingur in Thondai Nadu, the temple of Yoga Narasimha Swamy enshrines the Lord in the fierce yet serene man-lion avatara as its Moolavar, and it stands among the 108 Divya Desams celebrated by the Alwars. The place is anciently known as Thirukkadigai, a name that tradition derives from the sage Saptarishis and the swift attainment of grace here, the term "kadigai" evoking the brief measure of time in which the Lord is said to grant fulfilment to His devotees. According to the sthala purana, the sages who worshipped at this hill obtained release from afflictions, and the Lord appears in the contemplative yogic posture (Yoga Narasimha) rather than the wrathful form, signifying the peace that follows the destruction of evil. On the adjacent smaller hill abides Yoga Anjaneya (Hanuman), and the worship of Narasimha together with His ardent devotee is a distinctive feature of this kshetra. The temple was sung in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham by Pey Alwar, one of the earliest Mudhal Alwars, and by Thirumangai Alwar, whose verses extol the hill-shrine and its grace-bestowing Lord. The ascent of the hill, the abode of Narasimha above and Hanuman opposite, and the tradition of swift deliverance from sin and suffering together mark Thirukkadigai as a place of singular sanctity among the divine abodes of the Tamil land.

65.history: On the banks of the river at Thirunavai in the Malai Nadu of the Kerala region stands the temple of Navai Mukundan, where the Lord is worshipped as the Moolavar bearing the name Mukunda, the bestower of liberation, and which is counted among the 108 Divya Desams hymned by the Alwars. The very name Mukundan signifies the giver of moksha, and tradition holds this riverside abode to be a place where the Lord grants release to those who seek refuge. According to the sthala purana, this is associated with the celebrated episode of Gajendra, the elephant-king whom the Lord rushed to save from the jaws of the crocodile in the sacred waters, a deliverance that embodies the swiftness of divine grace toward the surrendered soul. Thirunavai is revered as a holy tirtha on the river, long honoured for ancestral rites and worship. The shrine was sung in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham by Nammalwar, the foremost of the Alwars whose Tiruvaymoli forms the heart of the corpus, and by Thirumangai Alwar, whose pasurams praise the Lord of this western abode. As one of the few Divya Desams lying in the Kerala country, Thirunavai holds a distinctive place in the sacred geography of the Prabandham, its Lord Mukundan revered as the compassionate granter of liberation to all who take shelter at His feet.

66.history: At Thiruvithuvakkodu in the Malai Nadu of the Kerala region rises the temple of Uyyavandha Perumal, whose very name proclaims the Lord as "He who came that we might be saved," and which is honoured among the 108 Divya Desams sung by the Alwars. The presiding Moolavar is worshipped as the redeemer who descends for the upliftment of his devotees, and the kshetra carries a deep association with surrender and rescue from peril. This Divya Desam holds a singular and tender place in the Prabandham, for it was sung by Kulasekara Alwar, the royal saint and author of the Perumal Tirumoli, whose intense devotion is poured into his verses on this Lord. Tradition cherishes here the famous theme of the child Kulasekara's heart, and the saint's longing to be born even as a stone, a step, or a bird at the Lord's abode rather than be parted from Him. According to the sthala purana, the Lord of Thiruvithuvakkodu is the protector who shields the helpless and grants refuge to those in distress, and the place is celebrated for the depth of the bond between the surrendered devotee and his Saviour. As one of the Divya Desams of the Kerala land, sung uniquely by Kulasekara Alwar, Thiruvithuvakkodu stands as an enduring emblem of saranagati, the loving self-surrender at the feet of the Lord who comes for the salvation of all.

67.history: Thirukkaatkarai, in the Malai Nadu (Kerala) cluster of the Divya Desams, enshrines the Lord as Kaatkaraiappan, a Divya Desam celebrated among the 108 sacred abodes of Vishnu. The presiding Moolavar is worshipped here as a manifestation of the Lord who reveals Himself to His devotees, and the kshetra is reckoned one of the Kerala (Malai Nadu) Divya Desams clustered around the ancient land of Chera. The temple's glory was sung by Nammalwar, the foremost of the Alwars, whose verses in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham consecrate this place within the sacred geography of Srivaishnavism. According to tradition, the very name of the Lord points to His readiness to grant the vision (darshan) of His form to those who seek refuge here, and the Alwar's hymns dwell upon the beauty and grace of the deity who is at once near and supreme. As one of the abodes that the Alwars praised, Thirukkaatkarai shares in the devotional heritage by which a place becomes a Divya Desam: it is not merely a temple but a stage of the Lord's self-revelation, hallowed by the song of the saints. Pilgrims who visit the Kerala Divya Desams hold this shrine among the sacred stations of their journey, and the continuity of worship here preserves the memory of Nammalwar's vision of the Lord of Thirukkaatkarai as gracious, accessible, and worthy of the soul's surrender.

68.history: Thirumoozhikkalam, set in the Malai Nadu (Kerala) region, is honoured among the 108 Divya Desams, with the Lord enshrined as Thirumoozhikkalathaan. This kshetra holds a distinguished place in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham, having been sung by both Nammalwar, the chief of the Alwars, and Thirumangai Alwar, whose combined praise marks the shrine as one greatly cherished in the Srivaishnava tradition. Tradition associates Thirumoozhikkalam with the Lord's grace toward His devotees, and the Alwars' verses extol the divine presence here as a refuge for those seeking liberation. The temple stands among the cluster of Kerala Divya Desams revered by pilgrims who journey through the land once known as the Chera country. As a sacred abode hymned by two of the greatest Alwars, Thirumoozhikkalam carries the special sanctity that comes from such twofold consecration in the Divya Prabandham; the saints' surrender at the feet of the Lord here, expressed in their Tamil hymns, continues to draw the devout. The Moolavar is worshipped with the devotion proper to a Divya Desam, and the place is remembered for its enduring association with the Alwars' vision of the Lord as compassionate and ever near to those who take refuge. Through the songs of Nammalwar and Thirumangai Alwar, Thirumoozhikkalam remains woven into the living fabric of Srivaishnava pilgrimage and praise.

69.history: Thiruvalla, known in the Divya Prabandham as Thiruvallavazh, is one of the 108 Divya Desams situated in the Malai Nadu (Kerala) region, where the Lord is worshipped as Thiruvaazhmaarban, the One who bears the auspicious mark of Sri (Lakshmi) upon His chest. The very name evokes the abode where the Goddess dwells eternally upon the bosom of the Lord, signifying His inseparable union with Sri Mahalakshmi, source of grace and intercession. This sacred shrine was sung by both Nammalwar, the foremost of the Alwars, and Thirumangai Alwar, and their hymns in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham establish Thiruvallavazh among the cherished Kerala Divya Desams. According to tradition, the place is hallowed as one where the Lord, adorned by the presence of His consort, extends His protection to devotees who seek shelter at His feet. As one of the abodes praised by two great Alwars, Thiruvalla holds an honoured station in the devotional geography of Srivaishnavism, and the meaning of the Lord's name here, the bearer of the radiant chest upon which Lakshmi resides, gives the shrine a special place in meditations upon the divine couple. Pilgrims to the Kerala Divya Desams revere Thiruvallavazh as a sacred station where the Alwars' surrender and song still echo, and where the Lord, ever accompanied by Sri, remains the refuge of all who turn to Him.

70.history: The Adbhuta Narayanan Temple at Thirukkadithanam, in the Malai Nadu (Kerala) country, enshrines the Lord Vishnu as Adbhuta Narayanan, the "Wonderful One," and is counted among the 108 Divya Desams revered in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham. The presiding deity is worshipped here in a serene, gracious form, and the shrine is reckoned among the cluster of sacred abodes lying along the Pamba river region of Kerala, several of which are associated in tradition with the five Pandava brothers, who are held to have installed and worshipped Vishnu at these places during their forest sojourn. Thirukkadithanam is traditionally linked in this manner with the worship of the Lord, the name Adbhuta Narayanan itself evoking the marvellous and grace-filled aspect of the Supreme One who dwells here for the redemption of His devotees. The glory of this Divya Desam was sung by Nammalwar, foremost of the Alwars, in his Tiruvaaymozhi, and by Thirumangai Alwar, the prince among the poet-saints, whose hymns secure the temple's place in the canonical corpus of the Tamil Prabandham. To be praised by these Alwars marks Thirukkadithanam as a place of high sanctity, drawing pilgrims who seek the darshan of Adbhuta Narayanan. As with the other Malai Nadu shrines, the temple preserves the distinctive architectural and ritual character of Kerala's Vaishnava tradition, while its standing as a Divya Desam binds it to the wider devotional world of the Alwars and the Sri Vaishnava sampradaya.

71.history: The Imayavarappan Temple at Thiruchengundrur, in the Malai Nadu (Kerala) country, enshrines Lord Vishnu as Imayavarappan and is honoured as one of the 108 Divya Desams celebrated in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham. Set in the sacred landscape of the Pamba river region, this shrine belongs to the group of Kerala Divya Desams that tradition associates with the worship of the Pandava brothers, who are said to have venerated the Lord at these abodes during their years of exile. The name Imayavarappan, traditionally understood to mean the Lord who is the bestower of boons, reflects the gracious disposition of the deity who abides here to grant the prayers of those who take refuge in Him. The glory of Thiruchengundrur was sung by Nammalwar, the foremost of the Alwars, whose Tiruvaaymozhi verses consecrate this place within the canonical body of the Tamil Prabandham and establish its standing among the sacred abodes of the Lord. The benediction of Nammalwar's hymns lends the temple enduring sanctity in the eyes of the Sri Vaishnava tradition, marking it as a place worthy of pilgrimage and contemplation. Like its neighbouring Malai Nadu shrines, the temple bears the architectural and liturgical imprint of Kerala's distinctive Vaishnava heritage, even as its inclusion among the Divya Desams joins it to the broader devotional vision of the Alwars, who beheld in such abodes the descent of the Supreme Lord into accessible, worshippable form for the salvation of all.

72.history: The Mayappiran Temple at Thiruppuliyur, in the Malai Nadu (Kerala) country, enshrines Lord Vishnu as Mayappiran, the gracious Lord of wondrous deeds, and is reckoned among the 108 Divya Desams hymned in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham. Lying within the cluster of sacred Vaishnava abodes along the Pamba river in Kerala, Thiruppuliyur is, by tradition, counted among those shrines associated with the worship of the Pandava brothers during their forest exile, who are held to have installed and adored the Lord at these places. The name Mayappiran evokes the divine mystery and beguiling grace of Vishnu, the supreme "Maya" through whom the worlds arise and are upheld, here made present in worshippable form for the sake of His devotees. The sanctity of this Divya Desam was sung by Nammalwar, foremost of the Alwars, in his Tiruvaaymozhi, and by Thirumangai Alwar, the prince among the poet-saints, whose praises together secure the temple's place within the canonical corpus of the Tamil Prabandham. The benediction of these two Alwars confers upon Thiruppuliyur an exalted standing in the Sri Vaishnava tradition, drawing pilgrims to the darshan of Mayappiran. As with the other Malai Nadu Divya Desams, the temple preserves the architectural and ritual character particular to Kerala's Vaishnava heritage, while its place among the 108 abodes unites it with the wider devotional world cherished by the Alwars and the Sri Vaishnava sampradaya.

73.history: The Thirukkuralappan Temple at Aranmula, known in the Tamil hymns as Thiruvaaranvilai, is one of the 108 Divya Desams and one of the cluster of sacred shrines along the Pampa river in Malai Nadu, the Kerala region. The presiding deity, the Moolavar, is worshipped here as Thirukkuralappan, a form that tradition connects with Vamana, the Lord who appeared as Kural, the dwarf brahmachari, to subdue the asura king Mahabali by asking for three paces of earth. In keeping with this association, the place is revered as one where the Lord measured out the worlds and restored the order of dharma. Aranmula stands among the divyadesams of Kerala that share the legend and sanctity of the Pampa, and it is held in high esteem by devotees of the region. The glory of this abode was sung by Nammalwar, the foremost of the Alwars, in his hymns of the Naalayira Divya Prabandham, and through his verses the shrine is counted among the celebrated abodes of Vishnu praised by the saint-poets. Tradition holds that the Lord here grants the boons of those who approach Him in surrender, even as He once approached Mahabali in humble form to accomplish the welfare of the devas. Worshipped with deep devotion across generations, Thirukkuralappan of Aranmula remains a living center of Srivaishnava piety, his sanctity preserved in the Tamil hymns of the Alwar and in the continuing reverence of pilgrims who seek the grace of the Lord who measures the three worlds.

74.history: The Paambanaiyappan Temple at Thiruvanvandur is one of the 108 Divya Desams, situated in Malai Nadu, the Kerala region, among the sacred Vishnu shrines associated with the Pampa river. The presiding deity, the Moolavar, is venerated here as Paambanaiyappan, the Lord who reclines upon Adisesha, the serpent of endless coils that serves as His couch upon the cosmic ocean. By this very name the shrine proclaims the abiding image of Vishnu as the supreme refuge who rests upon the great serpent while sustaining and protecting the worlds. Thiruvanvandur belongs to the group of divyadesams in Kerala celebrated for their antiquity and their place in the Tamil canon, and devotees revere it as an abode where the Lord grants peace to those who take shelter at His feet. The sanctity of this place was sung by Nammalwar, the chief among the twelve Alwars, in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham, and it is through his hymns that the temple is enshrined among the sacred abodes of Vishnu praised by the Alwar saints. As the Lord who rests upon the serpent couch, Paambanaiyappan embodies for his devotees the assurance of the Lord's protection and his readiness to awaken for the rescue of those who call upon Him. Worshipped with steadfast devotion through the centuries, the temple at Thiruvanvandur continues to hold its honored place among the Divya Desams, drawing pilgrims who seek the grace of the reclining Lord upon Adisesha.

75.history: The Anantha Padmanabhan Temple at Thiruvananthapuram is one of the most celebrated of the 108 Divya Desams, situated in Malai Nadu, the Kerala region. The presiding deity, the Moolavar, is worshipped here as Anantha Padmanabha, the Lord who reclines in yoganidra upon Ananta, the thousand-hooded serpent Adisesha, with the lotus bearing Brahma rising from his navel. This majestic reclining form gives the city its very name, Thiruvananthapuram, the holy city of Ananta, and the shrine is renowned as one of the grandest expressions in all the divyadesams of the Lord at rest upon the serpent in the cosmic ocean. The vast image, beheld through three doorways, has drawn pilgrims and rulers in homage across the ages, and the temple stands as a foremost center of Srivaishnava worship in the southern land. The glory of this abode was sung by Nammalwar, the foremost of the Alwars, in his hymns of the Naalayira Divya Prabandham, and his verses secure the shrine its honored place among the sacred abodes of Vishnu praised by the saint-poets. Tradition holds the Lord here to be the supreme protector who, in his serene repose, upholds and governs the universe, granting liberation and refuge to those who surrender at his feet. Worshipped with profound devotion and surrounded by enduring sanctity, Anantha Padmanabha of Thiruvananthapuram remains among the most resplendent of the Divya Desams, where the reclining Lord upon Ananta continues to receive the reverence of countless devotees.

76.history: The Adikesava Perumal Temple at Thiruvattaru, set amid the waters and greenery of the Malai Nadu region in present-day Kerala, is revered as one of the 108 Divya Desams, the sacred abodes of Lord Vishnu celebrated by the Alwars. The presiding deity, the Moolavar, is Adikesava Perumal, who is enshrined in a majestic reclining posture (sayana thirukkolam), his vast form filling the sanctum so that devotees behold him through three doorways. The name of the place is traditionally connected with the river that encircles and flows around the shrine, and the temple stands as one of the foremost Vishnu sanctuaries of the southern Malai Nadu, closely linked in tradition and proximity to the great Padmanabhaswamy shrine of the same region. According to the sthala purana, the Lord here is associated with the subduing of demonic forces, and his reclining form is venerated as the very fount of creation, 'Adikesava' signifying the primordial Lord with beautiful locks. Among the Alwar saints it is Nammalwar, the foremost of the Alwars and author of the Tiruvaymoli, who sang the glory of this divine abode, enshrining it forever within the Naalayira Divya Prabandham, the Tamil canon of four thousand sacred verses. Through his hymns the Lord of Thiruvattaru is established among the celebrated Divya Desams, drawing pilgrims who seek the grace of the reclining Perumal whose serene and immense form embodies the protective repose of the Supreme in the sanctified waters of this ancient land.

77.history: The Thiruvaazhmaarban Temple at Thiruvanparisaram, in the Malai Nadu region bordering the southern Tamil and Kerala country, is honoured as one of the 108 Divya Desams sung in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham. The presiding deity, the Moolavar, bears the evocative name Thiruvaazhmaarban, 'the Lord upon whose sacred chest resides Sri Mahalakshmi,' for the goddess of fortune is ever borne upon his breast, signifying that grace and prosperity flow inseparably from him. The Lord is enshrined here in a standing posture, granting darshan to his devotees with the assurance of the divine consort eternally at his side.
