According to tradition, the very name of the place, Thiruvanparisaram, is taken to convey the bestowing of boons and blessings, the Lord here being approached as the giver who withholds nothing from those who surrender. This sacred abode was sung by Nammalwar, the most exalted of the Alwar saints, whose verses in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham secure for Thiruvanparisaram its enduring place among the divine shrines of the Lord. As one of the Divya Desams of the Malai Nadu, the temple forms part of the small but cherished cluster of southern abodes that the tradition holds dear, and pilgrims journeying through this region come to behold the Lord whose very form proclaims the union of Vishnu and Lakshmi, the eternal source of auspiciousness and the refuge of all who seek him.

78.history: The Azhagiya Nambi Temple at Thirukkurungudi, in the Pandya Nadu country at the foot of the Western Ghats, is one of the most celebrated of the 108 Divya Desams. The presiding deity, the Moolavar, is Azhagiya Nambi, 'the beautiful and perfect Lord,' worshipped here in a unique tradition that venerates Vishnu in three forms across the locality, his beauty and accessibility drawing the devotion of saints and seekers alike. According to the sthala purana, this is a place hallowed by the Lord's intimate presence, and tradition holds it in special esteem as a shrine where the Supreme reveals himself with peculiar grace and approachability. The greatness of Thirukkurungudi is sung by no fewer than four of the Alwar saints in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham: Thirumazhisai Alwar, Nammalwar the foremost of the Alwars, Periyalwar the father-in-law of Andal, and Thirumangai Alwar the prolific singer of the Divya Desams. Their combined hymns mark this abode as one richly extolled within the four thousand verses, a distinction shared by only the most beloved of the sacred shrines. The Lord here is affectionately known as the one who is ever 'beautiful,' and the temple's traditions speak of his delight in the company of his devotees, including the legend in which he himself is said to have received instruction in the path of surrender. Set amid the verdant hills of the Pandya land, Thirukkurungudi remains a great centre of Srivaishnava pilgrimage, where the grace of Azhagiya Nambi is sought as the embodiment of the Lord's perfect and approachable beauty.

79.history: The Vanamamalai temple at Nanguneri, hailed in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham as Thiruvaramangai, enshrines the Lord as Thothadrinathan (Vanamamalai Perumal), one of the 108 Divya Desams sacred to Sri Vaishnavas and counted among the foremost shrines of the Pandya Nadu. The Moolavar stands in a majestic standing posture, gracing devotees together with His consort, who is revered here as Sirumamai Nachiyar (Varamangai Thayar), the divine mother from whom the place takes its Tamil name Thiruvaramangai. According to tradition, the Lord is celebrated as Vanamamalai, "the hill that is a garland of the forest," a name evoking His abundant grace and the verdant abode in which He resides. The shrine is sung by Nammalwar, the chief among the Alwars, whose verses in the Divya Prabandham consecrated this place within the eternal canon of Tamil Vaishnava devotion; through his outpouring the Lord of Thiruvaramangai is established among the abodes that the saint experienced as living presences of the Supreme. The temple is especially renowned for the sacred unguent of fragrant oil (thailam) that is poured over the Lord and never diminishes, a wonder cherished by pilgrims as a token of His inexhaustible compassion; the prasadam of this divine oil is treasured for its healing and auspicious power. As one of the great Pandya Nadu Divya Desams, Vanamamalai has long been a center of Sri Vaishnava worship and the seat of an esteemed Jeeyar matham, drawing devotees who seek the protection of Thothadrinathan and the maternal grace of Varamangai Thayar.

80.history: The temple at Sri Vaikuntam enshrines the Lord as Vaikuntanathar, affectionately known as Kallapiran, and ranks among the 108 Divya Desams of the Sri Vaishnava tradition, one of the celebrated cluster of nine Tirunelveli shrines (Nava Tirupathi) along the sacred Tamiraparani river in Pandya Nadu. The Moolavar graces devotees in a standing form, accompanied by His consort Vaikundavalli Thayar, and the very name of the place, Sri Vaikuntam, declares this earthly abode to be a likeness of the supreme heaven of Vishnu. The Lord's endearing title Kallapiran, "the Lord who is the great deceiver" or "the divine thief," recalls the sthala purana in which He is remembered for His playful and protective intervention on behalf of His devotees, stealing away their burdens even as He blesses them. This shrine is sung by Nammalwar, foremost of the Alwars, whose hymns in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham enshrined Sri Vaikuntam within the sacred corpus and exalted Kallapiran as a manifestation of the Lord of the highest realm made accessible upon earth. As one of the Nava Tirupathi worshipped along the Tamiraparani, Sri Vaikuntam holds a special place in the devotional circuit of the Pandya country, where pilgrims revere the river as the divine theertham and adore the Lord by His name that promises Vaikuntha itself. The temple stands as a luminous reminder that the Supreme Abode is not distant but graciously present, drawing the faithful to the feet of Vaikuntanathar.

81.history: The Vijayasana Perumal temple at Thiruvaragunamangai, known also as Natham, is one of the 108 Divya Desams cherished by Sri Vaishnavas and one of the Nava Tirupathi, the nine sacred shrines clustered along the Tamiraparani river in the Pandya Nadu. The presiding Lord is revered here as Vijayasana Perumal, the Lord seated upon the throne of victory, gracing devotees in a seated posture together with His consort, the Mother revered as Varagunavalli Thayar, from whom the place draws its name Thiruvaragunamangai. The very epithet Vijayasana proclaims the Lord's sovereign triumph and the assurance of victory He bestows upon those who take refuge at His feet. This shrine is hallowed by the hymns of Nammalwar, the chief of the Alwars, whose verses in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham consecrated Thiruvaragunamangai within the eternal Tamil canon and revealed the seated Lord as an object of his rapturous devotion. As a member of the Nava Tirupathi, the temple shares in the sanctity of the Tamiraparani, whose waters the tradition holds as a sacred theertham, and the pilgrimage to these nine abodes is undertaken by the faithful as a single garland of worship binding the Divya Desams of the region. Resting upon the throne of victory in the company of Varagunavalli Thayar, Vijayasana Perumal stands as the gracious bestower of triumph and refuge, drawing devotees of the Pandya country to behold the Lord whom Nammalwar sang.

82.history: The temple at Thiruppulingudi, in the Pandya country of the Tamil land, enshrines the Lord under the name Kaaisina Vendhar (Kaaisina Vendhan), a title evoking the splendour of a valorous and majestic king, and it is counted among the 108 Divya Desams, the sacred abodes of Vishnu celebrated by the Alwars. The Moolavar here is venerated in a reclining posture, the Lord stretched in serene repose as the master of all worlds, an aspect that draws pilgrims to behold his grace in this quiet village shrine. Thiruppulingudi belongs to the cluster of intimate Divya Desams gathered in the region around Thirukkurugur, the homeland of Nammalwar, and it shares in the special devotion that the saint poured upon the temples near his birthplace. It is Nammalwar, foremost among the Alwars and the author of the Tiruvaymoli, who sang the glory of this place in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham, and his hymns secure for Thiruppulingudi its enduring station within that sacred corpus of four thousand verses. Tradition holds that to worship at such shrines hallowed by Nammalwar's song is to draw near to the very heart of his devotional vision, in which the Lord makes himself accessible and approachable to those who seek refuge. As one of the abodes of this Pandya region, the temple preserves the antiquity and sanctity that the Prabandham confers, and devotees revere it as a place where the Lord, reposing in tranquillity, dispenses his protecting grace upon all who come before him in surrender.

83.history: Thiruthalaivillimangalam, in the Pandya country, is one of the celebrated 108 Divya Desams and is known as one of the two shrines that together form the Irattai Thirupathi, the 'twin sacred abodes,' a distinction by which two neighbouring temples are honoured as a single divine pair. Here the presiding Lord bears the name Aravinda Lochanan, the 'lotus-eyed one,' a name that lovingly recalls the beauty of Vishnu's eyes, soft and wide as the petals of the lotus, a form that has long captivated the devotion of the faithful. As an Irattai Thirupathi, this shrine is worshipped in close companionship with its paired Divya Desam, the two together drawing pilgrims who seek the darshan of both in one sacred sojourn. The glory of this place was sung by Nammalwar, the greatest of the Alwars, whose Tiruvaymoli enshrines Thalaivillimangalam within the Naalayira Divya Prabandham, and it is through his hymns that the temple holds its place among the abodes that the Alwars sanctified. Lying within the constellation of Divya Desams clustered near Nammalwar's native Thirukkurugur, the temple partakes of the intense, intimate devotion that the saint directed toward the Lord of these Pandya shrines. Tradition reveres Aravinda Lochanan as the Lord whose lotus gaze falls in compassion upon his devotees, and worship here, twinned with its companion abode, is held to bestow a fullness of grace. Thus the temple endures as a cherished link in the sacred geography sung by the Alwars, its sanctity affirmed by the verses of the Prabandham.

84.history: The temple at Thirukkulanthai, also known as Perungulam, in the Pandya region of the Tamil land, is one of the 108 Divya Desams hallowed by the song of the Alwars. The presiding deity is worshipped as Soranathan, also revered under the gracious name Maayakoothan, 'the divine dancer of wondrous play,' a title that celebrates the Lord as the master of mysterious and marvellous deeds, whose sport sustains and enchants the worlds. The Moolavar here is venerated in this temple set among the shrines of the Pandya country, where the Lord stands to grant his grace to those who seek him. Like the neighbouring abodes of this region, Thirukkulanthai belongs to the group of Divya Desams cherished near Thirukkurugur, the birthplace of Nammalwar, and it shares in the saint's deep and abiding devotion. It is Nammalwar, the foremost of the Alwars and the composer of the Tiruvaymoli, who sang the praise of this place in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham, and through his verses Thirukkulanthai is secured among the sacred abodes of that four-thousand-verse corpus. Tradition holds the name Maayakoothan to express the wonder of the Lord's hidden play, by which he conceals his infinite majesty within an accessible and beautiful form for the sake of his devotees. Worshippers revere Soranathan as the Lord who, through his marvellous grace, draws souls toward refuge at his feet. The temple thus endures as a sanctified abode within the devotional landscape that the Alwars consecrated, its glory affirmed by Nammalwar's hymns and its sanctity preserved across the generations of pilgrims who come before the Lord.

85.history: The Vaithamanidhi Perumal Temple at Thirukkolur, in the Pandya Nadu country of the Tamil land, enshrines the Lord as Vaithamanidhi Perumal and is counted among the 108 Divya Desams, the sacred abodes of Vishnu hymned by the Alwars. The very name Vaithamanidhi, traditionally understood as "the treasure that is laid up," expresses the Lord's character as the imperishable wealth safeguarded for His devotees, a refuge richer than any worldly fortune. Lying close to the cluster of nine Divya Desams gathered along the banks of the Thamiraparani river in the deep south, Thirukkolur is intimately linked with the life of Nammalwar, the foremost of the Alwars, who was born nearby and whose verses pervade this whole region. Nammalwar alone sang the glory of this shrine in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham, the collected four thousand verses of the Alwars, and his benediction has shaped its devotional fame ever since. Tradition cherishes Thirukkolur especially for its association with the celebrated saintly woman remembered as Thirukkolur Ammal, whose poignant utterances—recalling the great surrendered souls of scripture and lamenting her own lack of their fervour—have become a beloved expression of prapatti, loving self-surrender to the Lord. In her words the village stands as a teacher of humility before the divine treasure that Vaithamanidhi embodies. Pilgrims who tread the southern circuit of Divya Desams come here to behold the Lord who is the hidden, unfailing wealth of the soul, and to drink in the atmosphere of total dependence on grace that the place has long preserved.

86.history: At Thenthiruperai in Pandya Nadu, the Lord is worshipped as Magara Nedungkuzhaikkaadhar, and the shrine is venerated as one of the 108 Divya Desams sung by the Alwars. The unusual name of the presiding deity refers, in tradition, to the great fish-shaped earrings (makara kundalas) that adorn the Lord's long ears, an iconographic distinction from which Thenthiruperai draws its special renown. Set among the southern Divya Desams of the Thamiraparani belt, near the birthplace of Nammalwar, this temple shares in the deep devotional heritage that the Alwars wove around the rivers and villages of the far Tamil south. It was Nammalwar, the chief of the Alwars, who alone consecrated this place in song within the Naalayira Divya Prabandham, the four thousand sacred verses; through his hymns the form of the Lord wearing the makara earrings entered the perpetual liturgy of the Srivaishnava tradition. According to the lore preserved by devotees, the Goddess of this shrine is held in particular affection, and the place is counted among those where the divine consort's presence is especially gracious. The serene setting of Thenthiruperai, like its sister shrines along the river, has long been a station on the pilgrim path that winds through the nine Divya Desams of the region. Worshippers come to gaze upon the Lord adorned with the long, fish-formed earrings and to seek, through the mediation of Nammalwar's verses, the same nearness to the divine that the Alwar himself attained in this beloved country of his birth.

87.history: The Adinathar Temple at Alwarthirunagari, the ancient Thirukkurugur of Pandya Nadu, enshrines the Lord as Adinatha, the Primordial Master, and holds a place of singular eminence among the 108 Divya Desams. For this is, by hallowed tradition, the birthplace of Nammalwar, the foremost of the twelve Alwars, whose Thiruvaymozhi is revered as the Tamil Veda and whose verses form the very heart of the Naalayira Divya Prabandham. Nammalwar himself sang the glory of his native shrine, so that Thirukkurugur stands not only as a Divya Desam hymned by an Alwar but as the cradle of the greatest of them. The sthala purana holds that the infant saint, born here, remained in silent absorption and later, seated beneath a tamarind tree associated with the temple, poured forth the four works—Thiruviruttam, Thiruvasiriyam, Periya Thiruvandhadhi and Thiruvaymozhi—through which the whole Prabandham finds its summit. The town accordingly bears the name Alwarthirunagari, "the sacred city of the Alwar," in his honour, and Nammalwar is venerated here in image alongside the Lord. As Adinatha, the Lord is worshipped as the first and original being, fittingly presiding over the place from which the Tamil hymnal tradition itself sprang. Pilgrims on the southern circuit of the Thamiraparani Divya Desams regard Thirukkurugur as the central jewel, returning to honour both the Primordial Lord and the saint who, born in this soil, gave the Srivaishnava world its most cherished songs of surrender and love.

88.history: The Vatapatrasayi Temple at Srivilliputhur, in the Pandya Nadu region, enshrines the Lord as Vatapatrasayi, who reclines upon the serpent in the posture of cosmic rest, and is revered as one of the 108 Divya Desams sung in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham. The name Vatapatrasayi recalls the form of the infant Lord reposing upon a banyan leaf (vata-patra) afloat upon the waters of dissolution, the sustaining cause of all worlds. Srivilliputhur is hallowed above almost every other shrine as the birthplace of two of the Alwar saints. It is here that tradition holds Periyalwar, the garland-maker devotee, discovered and raised the divine child Andal, who grew into the only woman among the twelve Alwars. Periyalwar sang the glory of the Lord, and Andal, whose surpassing love made her long to be wedded to Vishnu himself, composed her Thiruppavai and Nachiyar Thirumozhi, the most beloved devotional verses of the Srivaishnava tradition, both associated with this sacred place. The temple is therefore cherished as the cradle of Andal's devotion and of the daily Thiruppavai recitation observed throughout the month of Margazhi. Its towering gopuram, an emblem of the town itself, and its annual festivals, especially the celebration of Andal's wedding to the Lord, draw multitudes of devotees. As the abode where the bride of Vishnu first offered her flower-garlands and her heart, Srivilliputhur holds an exalted and intimate place in the sacred geography of the Divya Desams.

89.history: The Nindra Narayanan Temple at Thiruthankaal, in the Pandya Nadu region near Srivilliputhur, enshrines the Lord as Nindra Narayanan, who graces His devotees in the standing posture, and is counted among the 108 Divya Desams celebrated in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham. The very name Thiruthankaal is traditionally understood to signify the place where the Lord, or the goddess, lingered and stayed, an abode of abiding grace. Tradition associates the shrine with the union of the Lord and His consort Sri Mahalakshmi, the goddess of fortune who is said to dwell here in special intimacy, so that the sthala is revered as a place where the divine couple bestow auspiciousness upon their devotees. The glory of this Divya Desam was sung by two of the Alwar saints: Bhoothath Alwar, one of the three earliest Mudhal Alwars whose hundred verses kindled the light of devotion, and Thirumangai Alwar, the prince-poet whose far-ranging pilgrimage and abundant hymns wove so many of the Pandya shrines into the sacred corpus. Through their inspired praise Nindra Narayanan stands among the perpetually sung abodes of Vishnu. Devotees come to behold the standing Lord whose upright form is held to signify His readiness to come to the aid of those who seek refuge, and to receive the grace of the goddess who tarries here. In its quiet sanctity near the famed shrine of Srivilliputhur, Thiruthankaal preserves its honored place within the constellation of the Divya Desams.

90.history: The Koodal Azhagar Temple at Madurai, the place revered as Thirukkoodal, enshrines the Lord as Koodal Azhagar, the surpassingly beautiful Lord of the meeting-place, and is honored as one of the 108 Divya Desams sung in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham. Madurai, the ancient seat of the Pandya kings and of Tamil learning, was traditionally called Koodal, the place of confluence, and the Lord who presides here bears the epithet Azhagar, the Beautiful One. A distinctive marvel of this shrine is its sanctum where the Lord is venerated in three postures, seated, standing, and reclining, beheld at different levels within the vimana, so that the worshipper may contemplate the manifold forms of the Supreme in a single sacred precinct. Tradition holds the place to be associated with the Lord's grace shown to a king, and with the assembly of devotion in the heart of the Tamil land. The glory of Koodal Azhagar was sung by Thirumazhisai Alwar, the profound sage-poet of deep philosophical insight, and by Thirumangai Alwar, the prince among the Alwars whose hymns garlanded the shrines of the Pandya country. Through their verses this abode in Madurai was sealed forever among the perpetually praised dwellings of Vishnu. Drawing pilgrims to its threefold vision of the Lord and standing within the celebrated city of the south, the Koodal Azhagar Temple holds a cherished place among the Divya Desams of Pandya Nadu.

10.history: Thiruppullam Boothangudi, in the Chola country near the sacred Kaveri delta, is one of the 108 Divya Desams, the holy abodes of Lord Vishnu hymned by the Alwars. The presiding deity (Moolavar) is worshipped here as Valvil Raman, that is, Rama bearing the mighty bow (val vil), recalling the Lord in his incarnation as Sri Rama, the prince of Ayodhya and slayer of evil. The very name of the place is associated in tradition with Jatayu, the great eagle-king who gave his life striving to rescue Sita from Ravana; the sthala purana holds that this region is linked to the bird-devotee whose supreme sacrifice Rama honoured, and the suffix 'pullam' (bird) is taken to commemorate this association. Devotees revere the Lord here as the gracious king who upholds righteousness and gives liberation even to a faithful bird. Among the Alwar saints it was Thirumangai Alwar, the warrior-poet and last of the twelve Alwars, who sang the glory of this shrine in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham, securing its place forever in the sacred Tamil canon of Sri Vaishnavism. Through his verses the temple is counted among the cherished delta shrines where the Lord reclines and reigns close to the life-giving waters of the Kaveri. Pilgrims who visit Thiruppullam Boothangudi pray to Valvil Raman for protection, for the strength to walk the path of dharma, and for the loving grace that Sri Rama is said to bestow upon all who take refuge in him.

11.history: Thiruaadhanur, situated in the fertile Chola Nadu amid the shrines of the Kaveri basin, is one of the 108 Divya Desams celebrated by the Alwars of Sri Vaishnavism. The presiding deity (Moolavar) is worshipped here as Aandalakkum Ayyan, a name expressing the Lord as the master who 'rules and measures'—the sovereign who governs and protects his devotees with sovereign grace. As with the great Trivikrama tradition, the epithet evokes the Lord who measured the worlds and who reigns over all beings, here gracing the small but holy settlement of Aadhanur. The temple stands among the closely-clustered Divya Desams of the Kumbakonam region, where the Lord is enshrined in serene majesty for the worship of pilgrims who traverse the sacred delta. Of the Alwar saints, it was Thirumangai Alwar, the soldier-poet renowned for the vast number of Divya Desams he visited and sang, who immortalised this shrine in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham, the four-thousand-verse corpus that forms the scriptural heart of the tradition. By his hymns Thiruaadhanur is enrolled among the eternal abodes of the Lord, deserving of the same reverence as the most renowned temples. According to tradition the Mother Lakshmi too is worshipped here beside the Lord, and devotees seek at this shrine the bestowal of prosperity, protection and ultimate refuge. Pilgrims approach Aandalakkum Ayyan with the prayer that the Lord who measures and rules all things may also measure out his boundless compassion upon them.

12.history: Thirukkudanthai, the sacred town of Kumbakonam in the heart of Chola Nadu, is among the most celebrated of the 108 Divya Desams, and its temple of Sarangapani is one of the foremost Vishnu shrines of the Tamil land. The presiding deity (Moolavar) is worshipped as Sarangapani, the Lord who bears the great bow Sarnga, reclining in regal repose; the sanctum is famously fashioned in the form of a grand chariot drawn by horses and elephants, evoking the descent of the Lord to earth. According to the sthala purana, the Lord manifested here for the sage Hemarishi, and the place is linked to the divine 'kumbha' or pot of nectar from which the town takes its name, marking it as a holy site where the waters of creation and grace are said to gather. Thirukkudanthai holds extraordinary distinction in the Prabandham, for it was sung by a remarkable assembly of Alwars: the first three Mudhal Alwars—Bhoothath Alwar and Pey Alwar—together with Thirumazhisai Alwar, the great Nammalwar whose Tiruvaymozhi is the soul of the canon, Periyalwar, his foster-daughter Andal the only woman Alwar, and Thirumangai Alwar. This rare concourse of saintly voices marks the shrine as supremely beloved within the Naalayira Divya Prabandham. Pilgrims revere Sarangapani as a master of compassion and the consort of Komalavalli Thayar, and the temple's grand festivals, especially in the Tamil month of Chithirai, draw devotees in great multitudes to behold the Lord who reclines in his celestial chariot at Kumbakonam.

91.history: Thirumaliruncholai, popularly known as Azhagar Koil, enshrines the Lord as Kallazhagar (Sundararaja Perumal), and stands among the foremost of the 108 Divya Desams sung by the Alwars. Set amid a verdant hill cluster of forest groves north of Madurai in the Pandya country, the temple is celebrated for the surpassing beauty of its presiding deity, whose very name Azhagar declares him 'the Beautiful One.' According to the sthala purana, this is a most ancient abode, hallowed by the Lord's perpetual residence upon the sacred hill, and tradition holds it to be linked with the great Vaishnava centre of Madurai through living festival custom. The glory of this kshetra is sung richly in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham: the early Mudhal Alwars Bhoothath Alwar and Pey Alwar invoke it, and Nammalwar, the foremost of the Alwars, pours forth his devotion to the Lord of Thirumaliruncholai. Periyalwar and his daughter Andal, the garland-giving saint, also sing its praise, and Thirumangai Alwar adds his verses, making this a temple cherished across the breadth of the Prabandham. The most renowned distinction of Kallazhagar is the great Chithirai festival, in which the Lord ceremonially journeys to the river Vaigai near Madurai, a procession that draws vast assemblies of devotees and binds together Saiva and Vaishnava traditions of the region. As a hill abode of luxuriant beauty, abundant groves, and ancient sanctity, Thirumaliruncholai remains one of the most beloved and frequently extolled of the sacred shrines of Vishnu in the Tamil land.

92.history: Thirumoghur, in the Pandya country near Madurai, enshrines the Lord as Kalamega Perumal, his form likened to a dark rain-laden cloud whose gracious bounty showers upon devotees, and it is counted among the 108 Divya Desams glorified in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham. According to the sthala purana, this kshetra is intimately associated with the act of divine guidance and protection: tradition holds that the Lord here grants safe passage to the soul on its journey to the supreme abode, and the place is venerated for the Lord's role as the one who leads and shields the devotee. A celebrated feature of this temple is its association with Sudarshana, the discus of the Lord, worshipped here in a distinctive form, and Thirumoghur is renowned as a kshetra where the protective grace of the Lord's weapon is especially invoked. The shrine is hymned by Nammalwar, the foremost of the Alwars, whose verses extol the Lord of Thirumoghur, and by Thirumangai Alwar, who likewise sings its glory in the Prabandham. Through their sacred compositions this abode took its enduring place within the Tamil Vaishnava canon. As the Lord of compassion who, dark and majestic as a monsoon cloud, pours out his mercy and ushers the faithful toward liberation, Kalamega Perumal of Thirumoghur is cherished as a refuge and a guide, and the temple endures as a hallowed station on the devotional landscape of the Pandya Nadu Divya Desams.

93.history: Thirukkottiyur, in the Pandya country, enshrines the Lord as Sowmya Narayana Perumal, the serene and gracious form of Narayana, and ranks among the 108 Divya Desams sung by the Alwars in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham. According to the sthala purana, this is an ancient and deeply revered abode where the Lord manifests in his peaceful aspect, and the place is honoured in tradition as a centre of great sanctity in the Vaishnava world. Thirukkottiyur holds a singular place in the later history of Srivaishnavism as the abode associated with the teacher Thirukkottiyur Nambi, from whom the great acharya Ramanuja is traditionally said to have received, after repeated entreaty, the sacred esoteric meaning of the Tirumantra; this living memory lends the kshetra a profound importance in the sampradaya beyond its Prabandham glory. The shrine is hymned by the early Mudhal Alwars Bhoothath Alwar and Pey Alwar, by Thirumazhisai Alwar, by Periyalwar, and by Thirumangai Alwar, whose verses together establish its standing within the divine corpus. Worshipped as the calm and benevolent Narayana attended by his consort, the Lord of Thirukkottiyur is revered for bestowing tranquillity and grace upon those who seek refuge. As a temple woven into both the hymns of the Alwars and the foundational tradition of Ramanuja's teaching, Thirukkottiyur remains one of the most spiritually significant of the Pandya Nadu Divya Desams, cherished by devotees and acharyas alike.

94.history: The Adi Jagannatha Perumal Temple at Thiruppullani, in the Pandya Nadu country near the coast of Ramanathapuram, enshrines the Lord as Adi Jagannatha, here revered in a special reclining form as Darbhasayana Rama, the Lord who lay upon a bed of darbha (kusa) grass. It is counted among the 108 Divya Desams sung in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham. According to the sthala purana, this is the sacred shore associated with the Ramayana, where Sri Rama, seeking passage across the ocean to Lanka, lay in penance upon a seat of darbha grass facing the sea to petition Varuna, the lord of the waters, to grant a way across. Tradition therefore venerates Thiruppullani as the place where the Lord prepared for the building of the great causeway and the rescue of Sita, and pilgrims associate the locale with Rama's resolve and grace. The temple's glory was sung by Thirumangai Alwar, whose Tamil verses in the Divya Prabandham enshrine this abode among the sacred shrines of the Pandya region. The reclining and the standing forms of the Lord here, together with the strong Ramayana association, give Thiruppullani a distinctive place among the southern Divya Desams, drawing devotees who come to worship the Lord who, though the sovereign of all worlds, humbled himself upon a bed of grass for the sake of dharma.

95.history: The Sathyamoorthy Perumal Temple at Thirumeyyam, in the Pandya Nadu region near Pudukkottai, enshrines the presiding Lord as Sathyamoorthy (Satyagiri Natha), and stands among the 108 Divya Desams celebrated in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham. The shrine is renowned for its setting amid a great rock, and the Lord is worshipped here in a majestic reclining posture upon the serpent Adisesha, a form that draws pilgrims to behold the cosmic repose of Vishnu carved into the living hill. The place name itself, associated with truth (satya/mey), reflects the tradition that this is the abode of the Lord who is Truth embodied, Sathyamoorthy. The temple's glory was hymned by Thirumangai Alwar, whose verses in the Divya Prabandham include this shrine among the sacred abodes of the Pandya country, securing its place in the canon sung by the Alwars. Set within and around its rock-cut sanctity, with the Lord reposing upon the serpent-couch, Thirumeyyam offers devotees the vision of Vishnu in his serene, all-sustaining form, and its enduring renown as a Divya Desam rests upon the grace of Sathyamoorthy and the Tamil hymns that consecrated it for the faithful.

96.history: Thiruayodhi, the sacred Ayodhya of the north, is venerated as a Divya Desam where the Lord is worshipped as Raghunayagan, the chief of the Raghu line, that is, Sri Rama himself. As the celebrated birthplace and royal city of Rama, the seventh avatara of Vishnu, Ayodhya holds a place of supreme honour in the Vaishnava imagination, and it is counted among the 108 Divya Desams sung in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham. According to the Ramayana and the tradition flowing from it, this was the capital from which the righteous Lord ruled, the city that grieved his exile to the forest and rejoiced at his return, and the seat of the ideal kingdom known as Rama Rajya. The Alwars sang the glory of the Lord of Ayodhya with deep devotion, and this northern abode was praised in the Divya Prabandham by Nammalwar, Kulasekara Alwar, Periyalwar, Thondaradippodi Alwar, and Thirumangai Alwar, a remarkable assembly of saints whose verses bind the city of Rama into the sacred geography of the Tamil canon. Kulasekara Alwar in particular, himself of royal lineage, poured out his longing for the Lord of the Raghus. As one of the few Divya Desams lying far to the north and as the very cradle of the Rama avatara, Thiruayodhi stands as a hallowed abode where devotees adore Raghunayagan, the embodiment of dharma and the protector of all who take refuge in him.

97.history: The Devaraja Perumal Temple at Thirunaimisaranyam (Naimisaranyam), in the Vada Nadu or Northern Region, enshrines the Lord as Devaraja Perumal, one of the 108 Divya Desams sung in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham. Naimisaranyam is among the most venerable of all sacred forests in the wider Hindu tradition, celebrated across the Puranas as the great assembly-ground where sages gathered in retreat to hear and recite the eternal narratives of the Lord; tradition holds it to be a place where the merit of austerity and the recitation of holy lore is greatly multiplied. The very name evokes a primeval forest set apart for tapas and the unbroken contemplation of Narayana, and the presence of the Lord here as Devaraja sanctifies that long lineage of devotion. Among the Alwars, it was Thirumangai Alwar, the warrior-saint whose pilgrim songs reach the farthest of the northern shrines, who sang the glory of this abode and secured its place within the Divya Desam canon. As one of the comparatively few Divya Desams set in the distant north, far from the Tamil country of most of these shrines, Naimisaranyam holds a special distinction: it binds the southern devotional heritage of the Alwars to one of the most ancient pan-Indian seats of sacred recitation. Pilgrims revere it as a place where worship of Vishnu and the hearing of His glories have continued from time immemorial, and where the grace of Devaraja Perumal rewards the devotee who comes seeking refuge in the forest of the sages.

98.history: The Vasudeva Perumal Temple at Thiruppiridhi, in the Vada Nadu or Northern Region of the Himalayan reaches, enshrines the Lord as Vasudeva Perumal and stands among the 108 Divya Desams hymned in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham. Set in the far north amid the holy slopes associated in tradition with the abodes of sages and ascetics, Thiruppiridhi is counted among that small and remarkable group of Divya Desams that lie deep in the northern lands, distant from the Tamil heartland where most of these shrines are found. Here the Lord bears the name Vasudeva, the all-pervading one, the indwelling soul of all creation, and devotees revere this form as a reminder that Narayana presides even over the remotest mountain sanctuaries. It was Thirumangai Alwar alone among the Alwars who sang the glory of Thiruppiridhi, his pilgrim verses carrying the devotion of the Tamil saints to these northern heights and enshrining the place forever within the Divya Prabandham. By his hymns the shrine takes its honoured station among the 108 sacred abodes, a witness to the breadth of the Alwars' vision, which beheld the same Vishnu present at the southern river-deltas and upon the snow-bound northern peaks alike. Tradition holds this to be a place hallowed by the austerities of rishis, where the devotee who reaches its remote precincts is granted the vision of Vasudeva and the blessing of having pilgrimaged to one of the Lord's most far-flung earthly seats.

99.history: The Purushothaman Temple at Devaprayag, known in the Divya Prabandham as Thirukkandam — sung as the 'kadi nagar', the fragrant and well-guarded city — lies in the Vada Nadu or Northern Region amid the Himalayan confluences of the sacred rivers, and enshrines the Lord as Purushottama, one of the 108 Divya Desams. The name Purushottama, the Supreme Person who transcends both the perishable and the imperishable, marks this as an abode where the Lord is adored in His highest aspect, presiding over one of the most holy meeting-points of the northern waters where, in tradition, the streams descending from the mountains unite on their long journey to the sea. This is among the rare Divya Desams set far in the north, distant from the Tamil country of most of the shrines, and its inclusion in the canon testifies to the vast sweep of the Alwars' devotion. It was Periyalwar — the great saint of Srivilliputhur, foster-father of Andal and singer of the Lord's tender glory — who hymned this abode, an unusual honour, for his verses most often celebrate the shrines of the south; here his song reaches the far northern heights to praise Purushottama. By his hymns Thirukkandam takes its place among the 108 sacred abodes. Tradition reveres the surrounding land as hallowed ground sanctified by the austerities of sages and by the sacred confluence of holy rivers, and pilgrims hold that to worship Purushottama at this remote northern seat is to behold the Supreme Person in the loftiest of His earthly dwellings.

100.history: Thiruvadariyaachiramam, the holy hermitage of Badari, is revered as one of the 108 Divya Desams and the most celebrated of the northern abodes of Vishnu, set high amid the Himalayas. The presiding deity, Badri Narayanan, is worshipped in a seated, meditative posture, and the kshetra is venerated as the place where the Lord undertook penance for the welfare of the world. According to the sthala purana, this is the eternal ashram of Nara and Narayana, the twin sages who are understood as forms of the Lord engaged in austerity amid the badari (jujube) groves that gave the place its name. Surrounded by snow-clad peaks and sanctified by the river that flows beside it, Badari is held to be a region where divine and ascetic life meet, drawing pilgrims across the ages on an arduous northern journey. Among the Alwars, Periyalwar and Thirumangai Alwar sang the glory of this distant shrine in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham, bringing the far Himalayan abode into the devotional landscape of the Tamil saints alongside the temples of the south. Thirumangai Alwar in particular, whose verses range widely over the sacred geography of the north, extolled Thiruvadariyaachiramam as a place where the Lord may be sought in His austere and gracious form. As a Divya Desam, Badari stands as a powerful reminder that the abodes sung by the Alwars are not confined to the Tamil country but extend to the very heights of the Himalayas, where the Lord abides for the redemption of all beings.

101.history: Thiruchaalagiraamam, identified with the sacred site of Salagramam, is counted among the 108 Divya Desams as one of the venerated northern abodes of Vishnu, lying in the high Himalayan region beyond the Tamil country. The presiding deity is worshipped as Mukti Narayanar, the Lord who bestows liberation, the very name proclaiming this as a place sanctified for the granting of moksha to His devotees. The kshetra takes its name from the salagrama, the naturally sacred stone revered in the Srivaishnava tradition as a self-manifest form of Vishnu, and the region near the source of its holy river is held to be the abode from which such sacred stones arise. According to tradition, the Lord is present here in a form especially associated with the deliverance of souls, and the place is approached by pilgrims as a remote yet profoundly auspicious destination. Periyalwar and Thirumangai Alwar sang of this northern shrine in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham, and through their hymns the distant Himalayan abode entered the sacred corpus revered by the Srivaishnava community. As one of the Divya Desams set far in the Vada Nadu, Thiruchaalagiraamam, with its presiding Mukti Narayanar, embodies the tradition's teaching that the grace of liberation flows from the Lord wherever He chooses to abide, drawing the faithful even to the snow-bound heights of the north in search of His redeeming presence.

102.history: Thiruvadamadurai, the northern Mathura, is honoured among the 108 Divya Desams as the sacred birthplace and childhood home of Krishna, one of the most beloved of all the abodes of Vishnu. The presiding deity here is worshipped as Bala Krishnan, the divine child, recalling the Lord's incarnation as the eighth son of Vasudeva and Devaki and His descent to relieve the earth of its burden. According to the puranic tradition, it was at Mathura that the Lord was born in the prison of the tyrant Kamsa, that His infancy and youthful exploits unfolded in the surrounding region, and that He at last destroyed Kamsa and restored righteousness. This intimate association with the Krishna avatara made Thiruvadamadurai especially dear to the Alwar saints, and it is sung in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham by no fewer than five of them: Nammalwar, Periyalwar, Andal, Thondaradippodi Alwar, and Thirumangai Alwar. Periyalwar and Andal, whose devotion centred so tenderly on the Lord as the cowherd child of the north, found in Mathura the home of their cherished Kannan, while Nammalwar, Thondaradippodi Alwar, and Thirumangai Alwar likewise extolled its sanctity. As the northern city of Krishna's birth, Thiruvadamadurai holds a distinguished place among the Divya Desams, uniting the deep Krishna-bhakti of the Tamil saints with the storied land where the Lord first walked the earth as the playful and gracious Bala Krishna.

103.history: Thirukkokulam, the Gokulam of the north sung in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham, is counted among the 108 Divya Desams of the Vaishnava tradition, with the Lord here adored as Navamohana Krishnan. Situated in the Vada Nadu, the northern region, in the cowherd country of Gokul along the Yamuna, this is the hallowed land associated with the boyhood lilas of Sri Krishna. According to the sthala purana and the wider Krishna tradition, it was in Gokulam that the infant Krishna was carried across the river and raised among the cowherds of Nanda and Yashoda, lifting the Govardhana hill, subduing the serpent and the demons sent to destroy him, and enchanting all who beheld him. The presiding deity's name itself, Navamohana, evokes the Lord who newly and endlessly captivates the hearts of devotees. The Alwars, though native to the Tamil south, sang the glories of these northern abodes of Krishna with intense devotion. Periyalwar, who beheld the Lord as the divine child, and his daughter Andal, who loved Krishna as her bridegroom, poured forth verses steeped in the sweetness of Gokulam's cowherd lilas, while Thirumangai Alwar too sang of this sacred place. Through their hymns the land of Krishna's infancy was woven into the sacred geography of the Divya Desams, allowing the devotees of the Tamil land to worship at the cradle of the Lord's most beloved earthly play.

104.history: Thiruthuvarakai, the Dwaraka of the north, stands among the 108 Divya Desams celebrated by the Alwars, where the Lord is worshipped as Kalyana Narayanar. Located in the Vada Nadu, the northern region, on the western seashore, Dwaraka is revered in tradition as the golden city of Sri Krishna, the kingdom he established after departing from Mathura. According to the puranic tradition, having led the Yadava clan to this coastal stronghold, Krishna reigned here as a king and householder, and it was at Dwaraka that he wedded his consorts; hence the presiding deity is hailed as Kalyana Narayanar, the Lord of the auspicious marriage. Dwaraka is honoured among the great holy cities of the land and is intimately bound to the close of Krishna's earthly sojourn. This abode was sung by several of the Alwars: Thirumazhisai Alwar and Nammalwar, foremost among the saint-poets, together with Periyalwar and his daughter Andal, whose hearts overflowed with love for Krishna, and Thirumangai Alwar, the prolific pilgrim of the shrines. Through their hymns in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham, the distant western seat of Krishna's royal lila was drawn into the devotional vision of the Tamil Vaishnava tradition, so that worshippers might glorify the Lord both as the cowherd of Gokul and as the crowned sovereign and bridegroom of Dwaraka.

105.history: Thiru Singavel Kundram, the celebrated Ahobilam set among the Nallamala hills, is one of the 108 Divya Desams and the foremost abode of the Lord in his man-lion form, where he is worshipped as Prahlada Varadar, the boon-giver to Prahlada, manifest as Narasimha. Lying in the Vada Nadu, the northern region, this hill shrine is hallowed as the very place where, according to the sthala purana, Lord Vishnu burst forth as Narasimha from a pillar to destroy the demon king Hiranyakashipu and to protect his devoted child Prahlada. So awesome was this manifestation that the place is said to have drawn the cry of wonder, Ahobalam, from the celestials who beheld the Lord's fierce and saving form. The sacred hill is renowned for its several shrines spread across the upper and lower reaches, each enshrining a distinct form of Narasimha, making Ahobilam a singular concentration of the Lord's man-lion glory. The name Prahlada Varadar enshrines the heart of the legend, for the terrible Lord who tore apart the demon turned with infinite tenderness toward the boy who had trusted in him. Among the Alwars, it was Thirumangai Alwar who sang the glory of this hill abode in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham, securing for Singavel Kundram its honoured place within the corpus of sacred shrines beloved of the Vaishnava tradition.

106.history: Crowning the seven hills of Tirumala in Vada Nadu, the northern region of the sacred geography of the Divya Desams, stands the temple of Lord Venkateswara, one of the most celebrated and most visited shrines in the entire Srivaishnava tradition. The Moolavar, worshipped here as Sri Venkateswara, Srinivasa, and Thiruvengadamudaiyaan, stands self-manifest (svayambhu) upon the hill, his form radiant and majestic, his right hand pointing toward his feet as the refuge of all who surrender. According to the sthala purana, the Lord took residence upon these hills in the Kali age to be readily accessible to all beings, and the seven peaks themselves are revered as forms of Adisesha, the serpent of the Lord. Thirumala holds an unrivalled place in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham, sung and extolled by the largest assembly of Alwars among all the Divya Desams: the three earliest mudhal Alwars, Poigai, Bhoothath, and Pey, together with Thirumazhisai Alwar, Nammalwar, Kulasekara Alwar, Periyalwar, Andal, Thiruppaan Alwar, and Thirumangai Alwar, all turned their hearts toward Thiruvengadam. Nammalwar yearned to become even a humble object upon the hill in his devotion, and Andal longed to serve there. The hill itself, often spoken of as the threshold between earth and the celestial realm, is regarded as the place where the Lord stands waiting to receive the surrendered soul. As a Divya Desam glorified by so many saints and held supreme in devotion across the ages, Tirumala remains the foremost living centre of worship of the Lord upon earth.

107.history: Thiruppaarkadal, the Ocean of Milk, is not an earthly shrine but a celestial Divya Desam, counted among the sacred abodes of Vishnu though it lies beyond the world of mortals. It is here, according to the eternal cosmology of the tradition, that the Lord reclines as Ksheerabdhi Nathan upon the coils of Adisesha, the thousand-hooded serpent, with Sri Mahalakshmi at his side, in the posture of yoga-nidra, the mystic repose from which the cosmos is sustained. The sthala purana of countless temples returns to this Ocean of Milk as the source from which the Lord descends to dwell upon earth, and as the realm from which the gods sought his help in the churning of the ocean that yielded amrita. The Alwars, in their hymns, again and again envision the Lord reclining upon the milky sea, and the reclining forms enshrined across the earthly Divya Desams are understood as reflections of this celestial repose. As one of the two non-earthly Divya Desams, Thiruppaarkadal stands as the cosmic resting place of the Lord, the abode between the Supreme heaven of Paramapadam and the manifold sacred sites of the earth, where the compassionate Lord lies ever watchful over the welfare of all worlds, ready to incarnate whenever dharma must be restored. Its inclusion among the hundred and eight affirms that the sacred geography of the Alwars embraces not only the soil of the Tamil land but the very heavens themselves.

108.history: Sri Vaikuntam, also called Paramapadam, the Supreme Abode, is the highest and most exalted of the Divya Desams, a wholly celestial realm beyond the reach of birth, decay, and sorrow. Here the Lord is worshipped as Sri Vaikuntanathan, Paramapada Nathan, seated in eternal majesty upon the serpent-throne with Sri, Bhu, and Neela Devis, surrounded by the liberated souls (nityasuris and muktas) and the eternal attendants who serve him in unbroken bliss. In the theology of the Srivaishnava tradition, Paramapadam is the goal of all spiritual striving, the realm of pure transcendence (nitya-vibhuti) toward which the surrendered soul journeys after release from the cycle of rebirth, crossing the river Viraja to attain the presence of the Lord. The Alwars, above all Nammalwar in the closing hymns of the Tiruvaimozhi, sang of this Supreme Abode as the final resting place of the soul, describing the ascent of the liberated and the joy of eternal service at the feet of the Lord. As one of the two non-earthly Divya Desams and the culmination of the hundred and eight, Sri Vaikuntam represents not a place upon a map but the very destination of devotion itself, the heaven of unending nearness to the Lord. Its place among the sacred abodes proclaims that the path traced through all the earthly Divya Desams leads at last to this Supreme Abode, where the soul abides forever in the blissful service of Narayana.

13.history: The Oppiliappan Temple at Thiruvinnagar, near Kumbakonam in the Chola Nadu, enshrines the Lord as Oppiliappan, also revered as Thiruvinnagarappan and Venkatachalapathi, and stands among the 108 Divya Desams sung by the Alwars. The presiding Moolavar is a majestic standing form of Vishnu, worshipped here with his consort Bhumi Devi, who at this kshetra is venerated as Bhoomidevi or Bhargavi. According to the sthala purana, the sage Markandeya undertook penance desiring the Lord himself as his son-in-law, and the Lord, accepting his young daughter, promised that the offerings made to him at this shrine would forever be prepared without salt — for which reason the naivedyam at Oppiliappan is traditionally offered saltless to this day. From this distinctive vow comes the name Oppiliappan, the "Lord without compare," matchless among all. The kshetra is closely associated in tradition with Tirupati, the Lord being regarded as a form of Srinivasa, so that worship here is held to confer the grace of the Lord of the seven hills upon the Tamil plains. The temple's glory is celebrated in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham, having been hymned by Pey Alwar, by Nammalwar, and by Thirumangai Alwar, whose verses praise the Lord of Thiruvinnagar and his abode. Devotees revere Oppiliappan as a bestower of well-being and prosperity, and the saltless offering remains the temple's most celebrated and beloved distinction.

14.history: The temple at Thirunaraiyur, popularly known as Nachiyar Koil near Kumbakonam in the Chola Nadu, enshrines the Lord as Thirunaraiyur Nambi, also called Srinivasa, and is counted among the 108 Divya Desams hymned by the Alwars. A singular feature of this kshetra is the exalted standing of the Goddess: the shrine is named for the Nachiyar (the Divine Consort, revered here as Vanjulavalli), and tradition holds that at Thirunaraiyur the Mother is given pride of place, the very name Nachiyar Koil meaning the "Temple of the Goddess." Here the Lord and his consort are worshipped together with special reverence for the Devi, and the deities are believed to bestow grace upon their devotees in abundance. The temple is also famed for the Kal Garuda, the stone vahana of the Lord, whose festival procession is held in great esteem by tradition, the vehicle becoming remarkably weighty as it is borne. This Divya Desam holds a distinctive place in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham as a kshetra sung by Thirumangai Alwar, whose hymns extol the glory of Thirunaraiyur Nambi and his abode. According to the sthala purana the place is hallowed by the worship of celestials and sages who sought the Lord here. Pilgrims revere Thirunaraiyur as a shrine where the intercession of the Goddess is especially powerful, approaching the Lord through the Mother who presides over this sacred ground, and the temple remains beloved for the union of the divine couple it enshrines.

15.history: The Saaranathan Temple at Thirucherai in the Chola Nadu enshrines the Lord as Saaranathan, also revered as Saranathaperumal, and is honoured among the 108 Divya Desams celebrated by the Alwars. The Moolavar is worshipped here with his consort, and the kshetra is held in tradition to be a place of great sanctity associated with the deluge that once covered the world. According to the sthala purana, when the cosmic flood threatened all creation, the essence of the sacred waters and the seeds of life were preserved at this place, and from this association the kshetra draws its standing as a refuge where the Lord protects and sustains the world. Tradition also connects the shrine with the worship of the sage Markandeya and with the gathering of the sacred theerthams, so that the place is esteemed for the holiness of its waters. This Divya Desam finds its place in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham through the hymns of Thirumangai Alwar, whose verses sing the glory of Saaranathan and his sacred abode at Thirucherai. The Lord here is revered as one who grants refuge and shelter to those who surrender to him, his very name evoking the protection (sarana) that the devout seek at his feet. Pilgrims hold Thirucherai in deep reverence as a kshetra where the Lord, having preserved the world through the great flood, continues to bestow his grace, and the temple endures as a cherished abode within the sacred geography of the Divya Desams.

16.history: The Bhaktavatsala Perumal Temple at Thirukkannamangai, in the Chola Nadu region of the sacred Tamil land, is counted among the 108 Divya Desams, the holy abodes of Sriman Narayana celebrated by the Alwar saints. The presiding deity, the Moolavar, is worshipped here as Bhaktavatsala Perumal, a name that itself proclaims the Lord's defining grace as the one who cherishes His devotees with a mother's tenderness. The very place-name Thirukkannamangai, evoking the dark, beautiful lustre of the Lord, points to His standing in a serene, gracious form to receive those who take refuge in Him. Tradition holds that the Lord here is especially the protector and beloved of His bhaktas, and the temple is regarded by devotees as a place where surrender (prapatti) bears its sweet fruit. Among the Alwars, it is Thirumangai Alwar, the prolific saint-poet whose verses crown so many of the Chola country shrines, who sang the glory of this Divya Desam in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham, securing its place within that fourfold corpus of four thousand sacred Tamil hymns. Through his paasurams, the form, the compassion, and the sanctity of Thirukkannamangai entered the eternal liturgy of the Srivaishnava tradition, and the temple has since drawn pilgrims who recite those verses before the Lord. Standing in this hallowed Chola heartland near the Kaveri delta, Bhaktavatsala Perumal continues to be venerated as a refuge for the faithful, the Divya Desam whose very names of place and deity together affirm the Lord's loving regard for all who call upon Him.

17.history: The Sauriraja Perumal Temple at Thirukkannapuram, in the fertile Chola Nadu region, is among the most exalted of the 108 Divya Desams, the sacred abodes of Sriman Narayana glorified in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham. The presiding deity, the Moolavar, is worshipped here as Sauriraja Perumal, also revered by devotees with the affectionate name Neelamega Perumal for the dark, rain-cloud beauty of His form. The very name Thirukkannapuram, the holy town of the dark-hued Lord, evokes His captivating presence, and tradition cherishes this kshetra as one where the Lord stands in surpassing splendour. The singular distinction of this Divya Desam is that it was sung by no fewer than five of the Alwar saints whose hymns make up the Prabandham: Nammalwar, the foremost of the Alwars; Kulasekara Alwar, the royal devotee; Periyalwar; his foster-daughter Andal, the bridal saint; and Thirumangai Alwar, who lavished many verses upon the shrines of the Chola land. To be celebrated by this great company of saints marks Thirukkannapuram as a place of extraordinary sanctity, for few among the Divya Desams received the praise of so many Alwars. Through their paasurams the form, grace, and majesty of Sauriraja Perumal were woven permanently into the four thousand sacred Tamil hymns recited in the daily liturgy of the Srivaishnava tradition. Pilgrims hold that worship here, accompanied by the recitation of these verses, brings the Lord's abounding grace, and Thirukkannapuram endures as one of the deeply venerated abodes of the Chola country, radiant with the devotion of the Alwars who sang its glory.

18.history: The Lokanatha Perumal Temple at Thirukkannangudi, set in the Chola Nadu region of the sacred Tamil land, is counted among the 108 Divya Desams sung by the Alwars in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham. The presiding deity, the Moolavar, is worshipped here as Lokanatha Perumal, the Lord of all the worlds, a name that proclaims His sovereignty as the supreme protector and master of the universe. The place-name Thirukkannangudi, the holy abode of the dark-hued Lord, points to His serene and gracious presence in this kshetra, where devotees gather to take refuge at His feet. Tradition reveres this shrine as a place where the Lord, though the very ruler of all creation, makes Himself accessible to the humble bhakta who seeks Him. Among the Alwar saints, it is Thirumangai Alwar, whose paasurams illumine so many of the Divya Desams of the Chola country, who sang the glory of Thirukkannangudi and thereby enshrined it within the four thousand sacred Tamil verses of the Prabandham. Through his hymns the sanctity, the form, and the compassion of Lokanatha Perumal were made part of the eternal liturgy of the Srivaishnava tradition, drawing generations of pilgrims to recite those verses before the Lord. Standing in the venerable Chola heartland near the Kaveri delta, the temple continues to be cherished as a Divya Desam where the Lord of all the worlds bestows His grace upon those who worship Him, faithful to the devotion first voiced by the Alwar who sang its praise.

19.history: The Soundararaja Perumal Temple at Nagapattinam, known in the sacred tongue as Thirunagai, enshrines the Lord as Soundararaja Perumal, the Beautiful King, and counts among the 108 Divya Desams glorified by the Alwars. The very name of the place, evoking the serpent (naga), and its situation upon the eastern coast of the Chola country have long marked it as a holy seat where the Lord grants His resplendent darshan to devotees. As one of the Divya Desams of Chola Nadu, the shrine belongs to the inner circle of abodes celebrated in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham, the four-thousand sacred verses of the Tamil saints. Its glory was sung by Thirumangai Alwar, the warrior-poet whose pasurams traversed the length of the Tamil land and carried the renown of many coastal and Chola shrines into the eternal corpus of the Prabandham. By his hymns the Lord of Thirunagai is reckoned a paadal petra sthalam, a place that has received the saint's song, and is thereby assured of perpetual remembrance in the daily recitation of the Srivaishnava tradition. According to received tradition, pilgrims to Nagapattinam approach the Lord in His form of surpassing beauty, beseeching His grace; and the temple, set in a region dense with Vaishnava sanctity, takes its place among the foremost of the divine abodes of the south. Here the faithful behold Soundararaja Perumal as the gracious sovereign whose comeliness gives the shrine its name and whose presence sanctifies the ancient port-city.

20.history: Thanjai Mamani Koil, the Great-Jewel Temple of Thanjavur, enshrines the Lord as Neelamega Perumal, He whose hue is that of the dark rain-laden cloud, and is venerated among the 108 Divya Desams of the Alwars. The name Mamani Koil, the temple of the great gem, signals the special esteem in which this abode is held within the Chola country, the heartland of Vaishnava devotion. The shrine carries a distinction shared by few, for its glory was sung by three of the great Alwars: Bhoothath Alwar, one of the earliest Mudhal Alwars whose mystic verses opened the way of the Prabandham; Nammalwar, the foremost of the saints whose Tiruvaymoli is honoured as the very essence of the Vedas in Tamil; and Thirumangai Alwar, the prolific singer of the Chola and coastal shrines. To be hymned by such a company of saints marks Thanjai Mamani Koil as a pre-eminent paadal petra sthalam, woven deeply into the four-thousand verses recited daily in the tradition. The Lord's name, Neelamega, the blue cloud, recalls the form of Vishnu that the Alwars never tired of praising, the dark and gracious refuge who rains down compassion upon those who take shelter in Him. According to tradition the place takes its identity from this jewel-like sanctity at the doorstep of the great Chola capital. Here the devout behold the cloud-hued Lord enshrined as the great gem of Thanjavur, and join their voices to those of the ancient saints in cherishing one of the celebrated abodes of the south.

21.history: The Jaganatha Perumal Temple at Thirunathan Koil, in the Chola country, enshrines the Lord as Jaganatha Perumal, the Lord of the Universe, and is counted among the 108 Divya Desams sung by the Alwars. As one of the sacred abodes of Chola Nadu, this shrine takes its place within the inner circle of Vishnu temples hallowed by the Tamil saints and recited in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham. Its glory was sung by Thirumangai Alwar, the warrior-saint whose far-ranging pilgrimage and abundant pasurams carried the fame of many Chola shrines into the eternal corpus of the four thousand verses. By his hymns Thirunathan Koil is established as a paadal petra sthalam, a place graced by the saint's song and thereby assured of perpetual remembrance in the daily worship of the Srivaishnava community. The Lord's name, Jaganatha, He who is the master and refuge of all the worlds, expresses the supreme sovereignty that the Alwars perceived in the deity of this place, who governs creation yet stoops in grace to dwell among His devotees. According to tradition the very name of the village, Thirunathan Koil, the temple of the holy Lord, preserves the memory of this divine indwelling. Here the faithful approach Jaganatha Perumal as the universal sovereign enshrined in their midst, and, echoing the verses of Thirumangai Alwar, cherish the shrine as one of the celebrated divine abodes of the Tamil land.

22.history: Thiruvelliyangudi, in the Chola Nadu, is reckoned among the 108 Divya Desams sung in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham. The presiding deity (Moolavar) here is known as Kolavilli Raman, a name that evokes the Lord bearing the beautiful bow (kol villi) of Rama, and He is worshipped reclining in His sacred abode. As one of the divine shrines of the Kaveri delta region, Thiruvelliyangudi holds an honoured place in the geography of Srivaishnava pilgrimage. According to the sthala purana, the name Velliyangudi is associated with the planet Sukra (Venus, Velli), who is held in tradition to have worshipped the Lord at this place and received His grace; for this reason the shrine is venerated by devotees seeking relief from afflictions and the favour of the Lord. The temple's enduring sanctity in the Tamil devotional tradition rests above all on the hymns of Thirumangai Alwar, the great warrior-saint and the most prolific of the twelve Alwars, who sang the glory of this Divya Desam in his Prabandham. Through his verses the Lord of Thiruvelliyangudi entered the canon of the Divya Prabandham, and the shrine is counted among the sacred places (mangalasasanam) hallowed by Alwar song. Pilgrims who tread the path of the 108 Divya Desams come to behold Kolavilli Raman in this quiet Chola country shrine, where the form of the Lord and the memory of the Alwar's devotion together preserve a living thread of the ancient Srivaishnava heritage.

23.history: Therazhundur, also known as Thiruvazhundur, in the Chola Nadu, is one of the 108 Divya Desams celebrated in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham. The presiding deity (Moolavar) of this shrine is Aamaruviappan, a name that tradition connects with the Lord who graced a cow (aa), and He is enshrined here as the gracious Vishnu of this sacred town on the banks of the Kaveri. Therazhundur is especially renowned in Srivaishnava memory as a place dear to the tradition, and it is honoured as the birthplace town associated with the great preceptor Sekkizhar in later Saiva tradition, though for the Vaishnava world its glory rests in its standing as a Divya Desam and in the song of the Alwars. The shrine's place in the Divya Prabandham was secured by Thirumangai Alwar, the warrior-saint among the twelve Alwars, who sang the praise of Aamaruviappan and thereby enshrined this town in the eternal canon of Alwar hymns. According to the sthala purana, the Lord here is approached as a refuge of compassion, and devotees revere His form with the mangalasasanam offered by the Alwar. Set amid the fertile Chola delta, Therazhundur preserves the atmosphere of an ancient temple town where the worship of the reclining and standing forms of Vishnu has continued across the centuries. For pilgrims of the 108 Divya Desams, the darshan of Aamaruviappan at Thiruvazhundur is cherished both for the beauty of the deity and for the living link it holds to the devotional outpourings of Thirumangai Alwar.

24.history: Thiruchirupuliyur, in the Chola Nadu, is counted among the 108 Divya Desams hymned in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham. The presiding deity (Moolavar) here is worshipped as Kripa Samudra Perumal, the "Ocean of Compassion," a name that beautifully expresses the Lord's boundless mercy toward those who seek His shelter at this shrine. The very name of the place, Chiru-puli-ur, recalls in tradition an association with sages and the tiger (puli), and the town is revered as one of the sacred Vishnu shrines of the Kaveri delta country. According to the sthala purana, the Lord reveals Himself here as the very embodiment of grace, and devotees come to immerse themselves in this metaphorical sea of divine compassion. The shrine's place in the Srivaishnava canon is owed to Thirumangai Alwar, the great warrior-poet among the twelve Alwars, whose hymns in the Divya Prabandham sang the glory of the Lord of Thiruchirupuliyur and thereby conferred upon it the sanctity of mangalasasanam. Through his verses this Chola country shrine took its place among the abodes that the Alwars hallowed with their song. Quiet and venerable, Thiruchirupuliyur continues to draw pilgrims who traverse the 108 Divya Desams, offering them the darshan of Kripa Samudra Perumal, in whose name and form the tradition celebrates the infinite, ocean-like mercy of Vishnu and preserves the devotional legacy of the Alwar who first sang His praise.

25.history: The temple at Thiruthalaichanga Nanmadhiyam, in the Chola Nadu region, enshrines the Lord as Nanmadhiya Perumal, and is venerated as one of the 108 Divya Desams sacred to Sri Vaishnavas. The presiding deity (Moolavar) is worshipped here in a form whose very name evokes the moon, and the sthala derives its name from this association: Nanmadhiyam carries the sense of the good or auspicious moon. According to the sthala purana, this is the kshetra where the Moon-god (Chandra) is said to have sought refuge and worshipped Vishnu to be relieved of an affliction, and so the place came to be known by the moon. Tradition holds that the Lord here graces His devotees in the manner of a cooling, healing presence, much as the moon soothes. Among the Alwar saints, it is Thirumangai Alwar, the prince of the Tamil poet-saints, who sang the glory of this shrine in his hymns within the Naalayira Divya Prabandham, the four-thousand-verse corpus of the Alwars; by his sacred Tamil verses the temple was sanctified and counted among the Divya Desams. Pilgrims journeying through the cluster of Divya Desams in the Cauvery delta of Chola Nadu hold this abode in particular reverence for its connection with Chandra and for the grace of Nanmadhiya Perumal, who is believed to bestow relief from distress and the calm radiance of divine compassion upon all who take refuge at His feet.

26.history: Thiruindalur, in the fertile Chola Nadu country, is home to the Divya Desam where the Lord is worshipped as Parimala Ranganathar, the fragrant Ranganatha, reclining in the serene majesty that recalls the great Ranganatha of Srirangam. As one of the 108 Divya Desams, this shrine holds an honoured place in the sacred geography of Sri Vaishnavism. The name Parimala, meaning fragrance, points to the tradition that the Moolavar here is suffused with a divine sweetness of presence, and the Lord is beloved as a form of perfumed grace. According to the sthala purana, this is a kshetra associated with Indra, the king of the celestials, whose worship of Vishnu here is reflected in the very name Indalur; tradition holds that the Lord granted His darshan to Indra and other divine beings at this spot. Thirumangai Alwar, foremost among the Alwars in the number of Divya Desams he celebrated, sang the praises of this temple in his hymns preserved in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham, the four-thousand-verse anthology of the Tamil poet-saints. There is a cherished tradition that the Alwar pleaded with the Lord here for darshan, and the Lord responded by turning toward His devotee, an episode lovingly remembered in the temple's lore. Through the saint's sacred verses, Thiruindalur was hallowed and enshrined among the Divya Desams, and to this day devotees gather to behold Parimala Ranganathar and to drink in the fragrance of his abiding compassion.

27.history: At Thirukkavalampadi, also known as Nandhipura Vinnagaram, in the Chola Nadu region, the Lord is enshrined as Gopala Krishnan, the cowherd Krishna, and the temple stands as one of the 108 Divya Desams revered by Sri Vaishnavas. The Moolavar here graces His devotees in the beloved form of Krishna, the divine herdsman of Brindavan, recalling the Lord's earthly leelas amid the cowherds and milkmaids. The appellation Vinnagaram, meaning a celestial city or temple of the heavens, marks this as one of the shrines whose very name proclaims its sanctity as an abode of the Lord. According to tradition, the place is linked with Nandi and with the worship offered here by celestial and divine beings, from which the name Nandhipura is drawn; the sthala purana cherishes the memory of Krishna's grace manifesting at this spot. Thirumangai Alwar, the great prince among the Alwar saints who sang of more Divya Desams than any other, glorified this temple in his hymns within the Naalayira Divya Prabandham, the four-thousand-verse compendium of the Alwars' devotion. By the sanctifying touch of his Tamil verses, Thirukkavalampadi was numbered among the Divya Desams and drawn into the sacred circuit of the Cauvery delta shrines. Pilgrims come here to behold Gopala Krishnan, seeking the tender, playful grace of the cowherd Lord who is held to draw near to His devotees with the same intimacy he showed the cowherds of old.

28.history: The Trivikrama Narayanar Temple at Seerkazhi, known in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham as Kazhicheerama Vinnagaram, is one of the 108 Divya Desams revered by the Alwars, situated in the Chola Nadu country of Tamil Nadu. The presiding deity (Moolavar) is Trivikrama Narayana, the Lord worshipped in the towering form of Trivikrama, the gigantic stride of Vishnu. According to the sthala purana, this form recalls the celebrated Vamana avatara, in which the Lord, having appeared as a dwarf brahmana before the generous king Mahabali, expanded into the cosmic Trivikrama and measured the three worlds in three strides, thereby restoring the order of the universe and subduing the king's pride through grace. The temple shares its sacred ground with the renowned Saiva shrine of Seerkazhi, the birthplace of Thirugnana Sambandar, making this a town of deep devotional significance to both traditions. Among the Alwars, it is Thirumangai Alwar, foremost among the saints in the number of Divya Desams he sang, who hallowed this shrine with his Tamil verses, securing its place forever within the Divya Prabandham. The town of Seerkazhi has long been celebrated in the religious life of the Chola country, and the worship of Vishnu as Trivikrama here invites devotees to contemplate the Lord who, though approaching in humility, encompasses all the worlds. As one of the 108 sacred abodes, Kazhicheerama Vinnagaram stands as a place where the boundless majesty of Narayana is made manifest, drawing pilgrims to behold the Lord who fills heaven and earth.

29.history: The Kudamaadu Koothan Temple at Thiruarimeya Vinnagaram, set in the sacred cluster of Thirunangur in the Chola Nadu region of Tamil Nadu, is counted among the 108 Divya Desams glorified by the Alwars. The presiding deity (Moolavar) is worshipped here as Kudamaadu Koothan, a name that evokes the Lord Krishna who danced the Kudakkoothu, the celebrated dance with pots, displaying the playful and enchanting nature of the divine. This shrine belongs to the renowned group of eleven Vishnu temples that adorn Thirunangur, the Thirunangur Vinnagarams, each bearing the suffix Vinnagaram and each sung within the Naalayira Divya Prabandham. Tradition holds these neighbouring abodes to be intimately connected in sanctity, and pilgrims journey among them to receive the grace of the Lord in His many forms. It was Thirumangai Alwar, the saint who sang the praises of more Divya Desams than any other and whose devotion is deeply bound to the Thirunangur shrines, who hallowed Thiruarimeya Vinnagaram with his hymns, thereby enshrining it within the sacred canon of the Alwars. As one of the 108 sacred abodes of Vishnu, this temple offers devotees the vision of Narayana as the gracious dancer and protector, and its inclusion among the closely set Thirunangur Vinnagarams gives it a special place in the devotional geography of the Chola country, where clusters of divine abodes invite continuous worship and remembrance of the Lord.

30.history: The Purushothaman Temple at Thiruvanpurushothamam, situated within the sacred precincts of Thirunangur in the Chola Nadu region of Tamil Nadu, is one of the 108 Divya Desams sung by the Alwars in the Naalayira Divya Prabandham. The presiding deity (Moolavar) is worshipped here as Purushothama, a revered name of Vishnu meaning the Supreme Person, the highest among all beings, in whom the fullness of the divine is beheld. This shrine forms part of the celebrated group of eleven Vishnu temples gathered at Thirunangur, the Thirunangur Vinnagarams, which are honoured together in tradition and visited by pilgrims as a connected garland of sacred abodes. The name Thiruvanpurushothamam marks this place as one dedicated wholly to the contemplation of the Lord as Purushothama, the perfect refuge of all souls. It was Thirumangai Alwar, the saint most abundant in the Divya Desams he praised and deeply associated with the holy town of Thirunangur, who consecrated this shrine with his Tamil verses, securing its place within the Alwars' sacred corpus. As one of the 108 Divya Desams, Thiruvanpurushothamam draws devotees to worship Narayana under His exalted name, inviting them to surrender to the Supreme Person who stands above the changing world. Within the cluster of Thirunangur abodes, this temple holds its honoured station, contributing to the rich tapestry of divine presence that distinguishes this corner of the Chola country as a land richly blessed by the descent of the Lord.
